## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [14 Marks] — Optical Transducers**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **photovoltaic cell**. Derive the expression for its open-circuit voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 0.75 at 850 nm. Calculate the **responsivity** and the **photocurrent** when the incident optical power is $10\,\mu\text{W}$. Given: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction and Working of a Photovoltaic Cell (7 marks)**

**Construction:**
- A thin p-type semiconductor layer is diffused onto an n-type substrate, forming a **p-n junction** near the surface.
- A thin metallic **front contact grid** is deposited on the p-side (allows light to enter) and a **back contact** on the n-side.
- An **anti-reflective coating** (e.g., Si₃N₄) is applied to reduce reflection losses.
- The device is packaged with a glass cover for protection.

**Working:**
- When light photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strike the p-n junction, they generate **electron-hole pairs**.
- The **built-in electric field** at the depletion region sweeps the electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side.
- This separation of charges produces a **photovoltage** across the terminals. When connected to an external load, a **photocurrent** flows.
- The cell is essentially a *current source* in parallel with a forward-biased diode.

**Derivation of $V_{oc}$ (already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$V_{oc} = \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\eta = 0.75$, $\lambda = 850$ nm $= 850 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $P = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ W.

**Step 1: Responsivity**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

Numerator: $0.75 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 850 \times 10^{-9} = 1.0213 \times 10^{-25}$

Denominator: $6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 3 \times 10^8 = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{1.0213 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} \approx 0.5137 \text{ A/W}$$

**Step 2: Photocurrent**

$$I_{ph} = \mathcal{R} \cdot P = 0.5137 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6} = 5.137 \text{ }\mu\text{A}$$

**Final Answers:** $\mathcal{R} \approx 0.514$ A/W, $I_{ph} \approx 5.14\,\mu\text{A}$ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B: [14 Marks] — Shaft Encoder & Digital Transducers (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **absolute shaft encoder using Gray code**. Why is Gray code preferred over natural binary code? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-bit absolute shaft encoder is used in a CNC machine. Calculate: (i) the number of unique positions per revolution, (ii) the angular resolution, and (iii) the digital update frequency if the spindle rotates at 600 rpm. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**(a) Absolute Shaft Encoder with Gray Code (7 marks)**

**Construction:**
- A circular disc mounted on the rotating shaft has $n$ **concentric tracks** of opaque and transparent segments.
- Each track is read by its own **photodiode + LED** pair.
- The segments follow a **Gray code pattern** (only one bit changes between adjacent positions).

**Working:**
- As the shaft rotates, the photodiodes generate HIGH/LOW signals based on whether transparent or opaque segments are between them and the LEDs.
- The parallel $n$-bit output is fed to a decoder or directly to a digital controller.
- Each angular position of the shaft produces a *unique* $n$-bit code, giving the **absolute angular position** without needing any reference.

**Why Gray Code?**
- In natural binary, transitioning from 0111 to 1000 changes **all 4 bits simultaneously**. If the photodiodes are read mid-transition, a completely wrong code (e.g., 0000, 1111) may be registered.
- In **Gray code, only ONE bit changes** per step. So a misread can at most produce a neighboring position's code — a small, bounded error of $\pm 1$ LSB.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $n = 10$ bits, $\omega = 600$ rpm.

**(i) Unique positions:**

$$P = 2^n = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ positions/revolution}$$

**(ii) Angular resolution:**

$$\theta = \frac{360°}{1024} = 0.3516° \text{ per step}$$

**(iii) Digital update frequency:**

Convert rpm to rev/s: $600 / 60 = 10$ rev/s.

Each revolution generates 1024 code changes, so:

$$f = 10 \cdot 1024 = 10{,}240 \text{ code changes/second} = 10.24 \text{ kHz}$$

**Final Answers:** $P = 1024$, $\theta \approx 0.352°$, $f = 10.24$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the three photoelectric effects** — photoconductive, photovoltaic, and photoemissive are *different* phenomena; do not interchange their equations.
> 2. **Forgetting units in $V_{oc}$ derivation** — the ratio $kT/q$ equals **25.85 mV at 300 K** (the thermal voltage); examiners expect this value to appear explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing $2^n$ vs $2^n - 1$** in resolution formulas — the *number of intervals* is $2^n$, but the *number of unique codes* is also $2^n$ (from 0 to $2^n - 1$).
> 4. **Not explaining *why* Gray code is used** — merely stating "it changes one bit at a time" without linking it to *read errors during transition* loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **GM counter dead time** — students often forget to *subtract* the dead time product from 1; sign errors are common.
> 6. **In shaft encoder problems**, students confuse "pulses per revolution" with "unique positions per revolution" — these are equal for *incremental* but different for *absolute* encoders.
> 7. **Photodiode responsivity** — forgetting to convert wavelength from nm to m leads to answers off by a factor of $10^9$.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Optical transducers** convert light into electrical signals via the **photoemissive** (electron emission), **photoconductive** (resistance change), or **photovoltaic** (EMF generation) effect.
- **Einstein's photoelectric equation** $E_k = h\nu - \phi$ is the *master equation* for photoemissive cells.
- **Photodiode responsivity** $\mathcal{R} = \eta q \lambda / hc$ gives A/W output per unit optical power.
- **Ionization transducers** use ionizing radiation in a gas-filled chamber; their behavior splits into **three regions** based on applied voltage: **Ionization Chamber → Proportional Counter → Geiger-Müller Counter**.
- **GM counter dead time** must be corrected using $N_{\text{true}} = N_{\text{obs}} / (1 - N_{\text{obs}} \tau)$ for accurate high-rate measurements.
- **Digital transducers** produce *discrete* output directly compatible with digital systems, eliminating the need for an ADC.
- **Shaft encoders** are the most common digital transducer for *angular position* measurement.
- **Incremental encoders** give *relative* position; **absolute encoders** give *absolute* position with a unique code per step.
- **Gray code** is preferred in absolute encoders because it changes **only one bit per step**, preventing false codes during transition.
- **Resolution** of an $n$-bit absolute encoder: $\theta = 360° / 2^n$.
- **Update frequency** of an encoder: $f = (\omega / 60) \cdot 2^n$ (with $\omega$ in rpm).
- **Phototransistor** = photodiode + transistor amplification; output current is $(1+\beta)$ times the photocurrent.
- The **work function $\phi$** is a material property of the photocathode; the **threshold frequency** is $f_0 = \phi / h$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct and Indirect Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT611 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct and Indirect Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Methods of Measurement

In the science of **Metrology** and **Instrumentation**, the fundamental objective is to determine the value of an unknown quantity by comparing it with a known standard. The way this comparison is performed classifies the measurement process into two broad philosophies: **Direct Methods** and **Indirect Methods**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMRT611 - Module 3):**
> Methods of measurement are broadly classified as **Direct Methods** and **Indirect Methods**. Direct methods involve a physical comparison of the unknown quantity with a primary or secondary standard, while Indirect methods measure intermediate quantities and derive the desired quantity through mathematical relationships.

---

## 1.2 Direct Method of Measurement

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A method of measurement in which the value of the unknown quantity is obtained directly, without the need for any intermediate calculation, by comparing it against a standard.

In the **Direct Method**, the measurand is compared directly with a reference standard of the *same quantity*. The reading on the instrument gives the true value (or a very close approximation) of the quantity being measured.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you go to a grocery store and want to know the weight of rice. You place the rice on a **weighing balance** (analog or digital), and the pointer/dial gives you the reading instantly. You do **not** need to do any math, do not need to measure length, volume, or any other parameter. The scale directly compares the weight of the rice to a calibrated internal standard (like a spring or a load cell) and displays the result.

### Standard Examples of Direct Methods

- Measurement of **length** using a **vernier caliper** or **micrometer**.
- Measurement of **mass** using a **physical balance** (equal-arm balance).
- Measurement of **time** using a **stopwatch** or **chronometer**.
- Measurement of **voltage** using a **voltmeter** (with calibrated scale).
- Measurement of **temperature** using a **mercury thermometer**.
- Measurement of **angle** using a **protractor** or **bevel protractor**.

### Sub-Categories of Direct Methods

Direct methods are further classified into:

1. **Direct Comparison (Null Method):** The unknown quantity is balanced against a known value until the instrument reads **zero** (null deflection). Example: **Wheatstone Bridge** for resistance measurement, equal-arm balance.
2. **Direct Comparison (Deflection Method):** The unknown quantity causes a physical deflection in the instrument, and the magnitude of deflection is proportional to the measurand. Example: **Pressure gauge**, voltmeter, ammeter.

### Key Characteristics of Direct Methods

- **Speed:** Generally **fast** and provide immediate readings.
- **Simplicity:** Operationally simple; minimal computation.
- **Accuracy:** High accuracy when standards are well-maintained.
- **Cost:** Often more expensive due to the need for precision standards.

---

## 1.3 Indirect Method of Measurement

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A method of measurement in which the value of the unknown quantity is determined by measuring one or more related quantities and computing the desired value using a known mathematical relationship.

In the **Indirect Method**, the measurand is *not* measured directly. Instead, several other quantities that are functionally related to the measurand are measured, and the desired value is computed using a **formula**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Suppose you want to measure the **density** of a cube of metal. You do not have a "density-meter." So you measure the **mass** (using a weighing scale) and the **volume** (by measuring **length × breadth × height** using a vernier caliper), and then you compute density using the formula:

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$$

Here, density is **not measured directly**; it is *derived* from other measured quantities. This is an indirect method.

### Standard Examples of Indirect Methods

- Measurement of **density** by measuring mass and volume.
- Measurement of **electrical resistance** using **Ohm's Law** ($R = V/I$) by measuring voltage and current.
- Measurement of **temperature** using a **thermocouple** (EMF generated is measured and converted to temperature).
- Measurement of **strain** using **strain gauges** (resistance change is measured and converted to strain).
- Measurement of **displacement** using **LVDT** (voltage output is measured and converted to displacement).
- Measurement of **flow rate** using an **orifice plate** (pressure difference measured, flow rate calculated).

### Key Characteristics of Indirect Methods

- **Flexibility:** Can measure quantities that are physically difficult to measure directly.
- **Computation:** Requires mathematical calculation, often introducing computational errors.
- **Cumulative Error:** Errors from individual measurements **accumulate**, reducing overall accuracy.
- **Cost-Effectiveness:** Often cheaper than direct methods for complex quantities.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Summary at a Glance

| Aspect | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Compares measurand directly with standard | Measures related quantities; computes value |
| **Computation** | Not required (or minimal) | Mandatory |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slower (due to calculations) |
| **Accuracy** | Generally higher | Limited by accumulated errors |
| **Cost** | Often expensive | Usually economical |
| **Examples** | Vernier, Micrometer, Voltmeter | Strain gauge, LVDT, Thermocouple |
| **Error Sources** | Instrument calibration, observation | Calculation errors, sensor non-linearity |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Most modern transducers (LVDT, RTD, Thermocouple, Strain Gauge) inherently use **Indirect Methods** because they convert a non-electrical quantity into an electrical signal (voltage/resistance), which is then measured and converted back using calibration equations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Framework (Detailed Taxonomy)

The two methods are part of a broader classification used in **Metrology** as shown below:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Methods of Measurement]
    M0 --> M1[Direct Methods]
    M0 --> M2[Indirect Methods]
    M0 --> M3[Other Methods]
    M1 --> S1[Null / Zero Deflection]
    M1 --> S2[Deflection Type]
    M1 --> S3[Comparison with Standard]
    S1 --> E1[eg: Wheatstone Bridge]
    S1 --> E2[eg: Equal-arm Balance]
    S2 --> E3[eg: Voltmeter]
    S2 --> E4[eg: Pressure Gauge]
    S2 --> E5[eg: Thermometer]
    S3 --> E6[eg: Vernier Caliper]
    M2 --> F1[Functional Relation]
    M2 --> F2[Substitution Method]
    F1 --> G1[Formula-based]
    F2 --> G2[Substitution]
    G1 --> H1[eg: Density via mass & volume]
    G1 --> H2[eg: Resistance via V and I]
    G2 --> H3[eg: Unknown weight replaced by known weight]
    M3 --> O1[Absolute / Fundamental]
    M3 --> O2[Secondary Methods]
```

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis: Direct Method

### 2.2.1 Null-Type (Zero-Deflection) Direct Method

The unknown quantity is balanced against a known standard, and the indicator is brought to **zero (null)** position. The value of the unknown is determined from the value of the standard that produces the null.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. The measurand is applied to the measuring system.
2. A precisely known reference quantity (from standards) is applied in opposition.
3. The system is adjusted (or the reference is varied) until the **deflection indicator reads zero**.
4. At the null point, the unknown quantity = reference quantity.
5. The reading is taken directly from the calibrated standard.

**Why this is highly accurate:** At the null point, the indicator error becomes zero, and the accuracy depends only on the **calibration of the standard** — not on the linearity of the indicator.

### 2.2.2 Deflection-Type Direct Method

The unknown quantity produces a **physical deflection** (e.g., pointer movement, mercury rise, needle rotation) proportional to its magnitude. The deflection is read on a calibrated scale.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. The measurand acts on a sensing element (spring, bourdon tube, etc.).
2. The sensing element deforms/deflects proportionally.
3. A pointer attached to the element moves over a calibrated scale.
4. The reading on the scale is the value of the measurand.

**Why it is less accurate than null type:** Accuracy depends on the **linearity, calibration, and scale resolution** of the deflecting element.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis: Indirect Method

### 2.3.1 Formula-Based Indirect Method

The desired quantity $Y$ is a function of measurable quantities $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$:

$$Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$$

Each $X_i$ is measured using a direct method, and $Y$ is computed algebraically.

### 2.3.2 Propagation of Errors in Indirect Methods

For a derived quantity $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$, the **absolute error** in $Y$ is given by the **Total Differential**:

$$\Delta Y = \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial X_1} \right\vert \cdot \Delta X_1 + \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial X_2} \right\vert \cdot \Delta X_2 + \cdots + \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial X_n} \right\vert \cdot \Delta X_n$$

The **relative error** is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial X_i} \right\vert \cdot \Delta X_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Error Multiplication Rule:** If $Y = K \cdot X_1^a \cdot X_2^b$ (power form), then the relative error is:
> $$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \vert a \vert \cdot \dfrac{\Delta X_1}{X_1} + \vert b \vert \cdot \dfrac{\Delta X_2}{X_2}$$
> This is a **frequently asked KTU question** for both 3-mark and 14-mark problems.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Indirect measurement | $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ | $Y$ derived from measurable $X_i$ |
| 2 | Density (indirect) | $\rho = m / V$ | $m$ = mass, $V$ = volume |
| 3 | Resistance (Ohm's Law) | $R = V / I$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current |
| 4 | Absolute error (sum) | $\Delta Y = \sum \vert \partial f / \partial X_i \vert \cdot \Delta X_i$ | For $Y = f(X_1, X_2)$ |
| 5 | Absolute error (product) | $\Delta(X_1 X_2) = X_2 \Delta X_1 + X_1 \Delta X_2$ | For $Y = X_1 \cdot X_2$ |
| 6 | Absolute error (quotient) | $\Delta(X_1 / X_2) = (X_2 \Delta X_1 + X_1 \Delta X_2) / X_2^2$ | For $Y = X_1 / X_2$ |
| 7 | Relative error (power form) | $\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \sum \vert a_i \vert \dfrac{\Delta X_i}{X_i}$ | For $Y = K \prod X_i^{a_i}$ |
| 8 | Strain (indirect) | $\epsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Ratio of length change |
| 9 | Temperature (RTD) | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient |
| 10 | LVDT output | $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | $K$ = sensitivity, $x$ = displacement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The error propagation equations are **critical** in engineering practice. For example, when computing the efficiency of a turbine from measured input/output power, or when computing the stress in a material from measured strain, the engineer **must** account for cumulative errors to ensure the design is safe.

---

## 2.5 Comparative Strengths and Weaknesses

### Direct Method — Advantages

- **No computation required** — direct reading.
- **Less prone to operator error** during analysis.
- **Faster** for routine, repetitive measurements.
- **Higher reliability** for simple quantities.

### Direct Method — Disadvantages

- Requires a **dedicated instrument** for each quantity.
- **Limited range** — instruments saturate at extreme values.
- **Expensive** for precision (e.g., laboratory-grade balances).
- Cannot easily measure quantities that are **inaccessible** (e.g., inside a closed pipe).

### Indirect Method — Advantages

- **Versatile** — can measure complex quantities using basic instruments.
- **Cost-effective** — uses general-purpose sensors.
- **Can measure inaccessible quantities** (via remote sensors like LVDT, thermocouple).
- **Wide range** of measurable quantities.

### Indirect Method — Disadvantages

- **Computational overhead** — formula-based evaluation needed.
- **Cumulative errors** reduce accuracy.
- **Requires calibration** of intermediate sensors.
- **Non-linearity** of sensors can introduce systematic errors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Error Propagation in a Quotient (Density Example)

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem**. The measurand is density $\rho$ of a rectangular block:

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{m}{L \cdot B \cdot H}$$

where $m$ = mass, $L$ = length, $B$ = breadth, $H$ = height.

### Step 1: Express $\rho$ in logarithmic form

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \rho = \ln m - \ln L - \ln B - \ln H$$

### Step 2: Differentiate both sides

The differential of $\ln \rho$ is $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$, and similarly for each variable:

$$\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \dfrac{dm}{m} - \dfrac{dL}{L} - \dfrac{dB}{B} - \dfrac{dH}{H}$$

### Step 3: Convert differentials to finite (absolute) errors

Replace $d\rho \rightarrow \Delta \rho$, $dm \rightarrow \Delta m$, etc., and take absolute values (since the worst-case error is additive in magnitude):

$$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta B}{B} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta H}{H} \right\vert$$

This is the **maximum relative error** in density.

### Step 4: Multiply both sides by $\rho$ to get absolute error

$$\Delta \rho = \rho \left( \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} + \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} + \dfrac{\Delta B}{B} + \dfrac{\Delta H}{H} \right)$$

### Step 5: Generalization

For any function $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$, the **relative error** is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial X_i} \right\vert \cdot \Delta X_i$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Error in Resistance from Ohm's Law

Resistance is determined by measuring voltage $V$ across and current $I$ through a resistor:

$$R = \dfrac{V}{I}$$

### Step 1: Take logarithms

$$\ln R = \ln V - \ln I$$

### Step 2: Differentiate

$$\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{dV}{V} - \dfrac{dI}{I}$$

### Step 3: Take absolute values for maximum error

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \right\vert$$

### Step 4: Absolute error in $R$

$$\Delta R = R \left( \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} + \dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \right)$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Numerical Application

> **Problem:** A steel block has mass $m = 25.00 \pm 0.05$ kg. Its dimensions are measured as $L = 0.500 \pm 0.002$ m, $B = 0.200 \pm 0.002$ m, $H = 0.100 \pm 0.002$ m. Calculate the density and the maximum absolute error in density.

### Step 1: Calculate the volume

$$V = L \times B \times H = 0.500 \times 0.200 \times 0.100 = 0.0100 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2: Calculate the density

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{25.00}{0.0100} = 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 3: Calculate individual relative errors

$$\dfrac{\Delta m}{m} = \dfrac{0.05}{25.00} = 0.0020 = 0.20\%$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{0.002}{0.500} = 0.0040 = 0.40\%$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta B}{B} = \dfrac{0.002}{0.200} = 0.0100 = 1.00\%$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta H}{H} = \dfrac{0.002}{0.100} = 0.0200 = 2.00\%$$

### Step 4: Sum relative errors (worst case)

$$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = 0.0020 + 0.0040 + 0.0100 + 0.0200 = 0.0360 = 3.60\%$$

### Step 5: Compute absolute error

$$\Delta \rho = \rho \times 0.0360 = 2500 \times 0.0360 = 90 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 6: Final Answer

$$\boxed{\rho = 2500 \pm 90 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad \text{or} \quad \rho = (2500 \pm 3.6\%) \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Method

| Measurement Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Measuring diameter of a shaft in a workshop | **Direct (vernier)** | Quick, high accuracy, no computation |
| Measuring temperature inside a furnace (1500°C) | **Indirect (thermocouple)** | No direct contact thermometer survives |
| Measuring mass of a chemical sample in a lab | **Direct (electronic balance)** | High precision, easy calibration |
| Measuring flow rate of oil in a pipeline | **Indirect (orifice + DP transmitter)** | Flow is a derived quantity (Bernoulli's eq.) |
| Measuring strain on a bridge girder | **Indirect (strain gauge)** | Strain is computed from $\Delta R / R$ |
| Measuring voltage of a battery | **Direct (voltmeter)** | Instant, precise, simple |
| Measuring density of an unknown liquid | **Indirect (mass + volume)** | No direct density sensor is practical |
| Measuring displacement of a piston inside an engine | **Indirect (LVDT)** | Internal location, requires non-contact sensing |
| Measuring length of a steel rod | **Direct (micrometer)** | Sub-millimeter accuracy needed |
| Measuring pressure in a sealed tank | **Direct (Bourdon gauge)** | Real-time readable |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** Use the **Direct Method** whenever a direct-reading instrument is available at acceptable cost and accuracy. Use the **Indirect Method** when the measurand is inaccessible, extreme in value, or when the derived quantity offers a more useful representation (e.g., strain instead of elongation).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Methods of Measurement

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of measurement methods, showing the flow of information from the **measurand** to the **displayed result** in both direct and indirect paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Quantity / Measurand] --> B{Measurement Strategy}
    B -->|Direct Path| C[Apply Measurand to Sensor]
    C --> D[Sensor Output is Same Quantity Type]
    D --> E[Calibrated Scale / Display]
    E --> F[Direct Reading of Measurand]
    B -->|Indirect Path| G[Identify Related Quantities X1, X2, ... Xn]
    G --> H[Measure Each Xi using Direct Sensors]
    H --> I[Acquire Electrical / Analog Signals]
    I --> J[Signal Conditioning and Linearization]
    J --> K[Apply Mathematical Model: Y = f(X1, X2, ... Xn)]
    K --> L[Compute Y using Calibration Equation]
    L --> M[Display / Output Derived Quantity]
    F --> N[Final Result]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Error Analysis}
    O --> P[Instrumental Error]
    O --> Q[Computational Error]
    O --> R[Cumulative / Propagation Error]
    O --> S[Environmental Error]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Direct Method (Voltmeter Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Unknown Voltage Vx] --> P2[Voltmeter Coil]
    P2 --> P3[Magnetic Deflection Mechanism]
    P3 --> P4[Pointer Movement]
    P4 --> P5[Calibrated Scale]
    P5 --> P6[Direct Reading of Vx in Volts]
    P1 --> P7[Reference Voltage Standard]
    P7 --> P2
```

> **Interpretation:** The unknown voltage drives a current through a coil placed in a magnetic field. The magnetic force deflects a pointer proportionally. The pointer position, against a pre-calibrated scale, directly displays the voltage value. **No computation is required.**

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Indirect Method (Strain Gauge Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Applied Force on Structure] --> Q2[Mechanical Strain epsilon on Member]
    Q2 --> Q3[Strain Gauge bonded to Member]
    Q3 --> Q4[Change in Resistance Delta R]
    Q4 --> Q5[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    Q5 --> Q6[Output Voltage Vout]
    Q6 --> Q7[Amplifier and Signal Conditioner]
    Q7 --> Q8[ADC / Microcontroller]
    Q8 --> Q9[Apply Gauge Factor Equation: epsilon = Delta R divided by R times GF]
    Q9 --> Q10[Display Strain Value epsilon]
    Q9 --> Q11[Compute Stress: sigma = E times epsilon]
    Q11 --> Q12[Display Stress Value sigma]
```

> **Interpretation:** The strain is not measured directly. The **change in resistance** is the primary measurement, and strain is **computed** using the gauge factor. Stress is then **derived** by multiplying by Young's modulus. This is a **two-stage indirect measurement**.

---

## 4.4 Error Propagation Flowchart (Indirect Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Measurand Y = f of X1, X2, ... Xn] --> R2[Identify All Input Quantities Xi]
    R2 --> R3[Measure Each Xi Independently]
    R3 --> R4[Record Absolute Error Delta Xi for each Xi]
    R4 --> R5[Compute Partial Derivatives partial f over partial Xi]
    R5 --> R6[Apply Error Propagation Formula]
    R6 --> R7[Sum Weighted Absolute Errors]
    R7 --> R8[Compute Relative Error Delta Y over Y]
    R8 --> R9[Compute Absolute Error Delta Y]
    R9 --> R10[Report Final Value: Y plus or minus Delta Y]
```

---

## 4.5 Comparison Schematic: Null vs Deflection Method (Direct)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Direct Method] --> S2{Indicator Type}
    S2 -->|Pointer Reads Zero at Balance| S3[Null Method]
    S2 -->|Pointer Deflects Proportionally| S4[Deflection Method]
    S3 --> S5[eg: Wheatstone Bridge]
    S3 --> S6[eg: Equal-arm Balance]
    S3 --> S7[eg: Potentiometer]
    S4 --> S8[eg: Voltmeter]
    S4 --> S9[eg: Pressure Gauge]
    S4 --> S10[eg: Thermometer]
    S3 --> S11[High Accuracy at Null Point]
    S4 --> S12[Lower Accuracy, Depends on Scale]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Direct and Indirect methods of measurement. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Comparison | Compared directly with a standard | Measured by other related quantities |
| Computation | Not required | Required |
| Example 1 | Vernier caliper for length | Strain gauge for strain |
| Example 2 | Voltmeter for voltage | LVDT for displacement |

In the **Direct Method**, the value of the measurand is obtained by direct physical comparison with a primary or secondary standard, and the instrument itself displays the value without calculation. Examples: vernier caliper (length) and mercury thermometer (temperature).

In the **Indirect Method**, the measurand is determined by measuring one or more related quantities and computing the desired value using a known mathematical relationship. Examples: measurement of resistance using Ohm's Law ($R = V/I$) and measurement of density from mass and volume ($\rho = m/V$).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**State the formula for the maximum relative error in the resistance $R$ measured by Ohm's Law using voltmeter and ammeter. Explain the significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The resistance is computed as $R = V / I$, where $V$ = voltage and $I$ = current.

Taking logarithms: $\ln R = \ln V - \ln I$

Differentiating and taking absolute values:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \right\vert$$

**Significance:** The relative error in the computed resistance is the **sum of the relative errors** in the measured voltage and current. This is the *worst-case* (maximum) error. The absolute error is:

$$\Delta R = R \left( \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} + \dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \right)$$

This shows that errors in indirect measurement **accumulate**, and the engineer must use precise instruments for both voltage and current to achieve acceptable accuracy in $R$.

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a)** Explain the **Direct Method** of measurement in detail. Classify it into **Null-type** and **Deflection-type** methods with suitable examples. Discuss the relative merits of each. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A rectangular metal block has its dimensions measured as $L = 10.0 \pm 0.1$ cm, $B = 5.0 \pm 0.1$ cm, and $H = 2.0 \pm 0.1$ cm. Its mass is $m = 850 \pm 2$ g. Calculate **(i)** the volume, **(ii)** the density, and **(iii)** the maximum absolute and relative error in density using the **Indirect Method** of error analysis. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Direct Method of Measurement (7 Marks):**

The **Direct Method** of measurement is one in which the value of the unknown quantity (measurand) is determined by direct physical comparison with a known standard of the **same physical nature**. No intermediate computation is required to obtain the final reading.

**Classification:**

**1. Null-Type (Zero-Deflection) Method (3.5 marks):**
In this method, the unknown quantity is balanced against a known reference standard until the detector/indicator reads **zero (null)**. At the null point, the unknown equals the reference. The accuracy depends only on the standard.

- **Example 1: Equal-arm Balance** — Unknown mass on one pan is balanced by standard masses on the other. When the beam is horizontal, the masses are equal.
- **Example 2: Wheatstone Bridge** — Unknown resistance is determined by balancing the bridge so that the galvanometer reads zero. Then $R_x = R_2 R_3 / R_1$.
- **Example 3: Potentiometer** — Unknown EMF is balanced against a known voltage drop across a calibrated slide wire.

**2. Deflection-Type Method (2.5 marks):**
Here, the measurand causes a **physical deflection** of a pointer or indicator, and the magnitude of deflection (read against a calibrated scale) is proportional to the measurand.

- **Example 1: Voltmeter** — Current through a coil in a magnetic field produces a torque that deflects a pointer.
- **Example 2: Pressure Gauge (Bourdon Tube)** — Pressure deflects a curved tube, and the motion is transmitted to a pointer.
- **Example 3: Mercury Thermometer** — Temperature causes mercury expansion, and the height is read on a calibrated stem.

**Comparative Merits (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Null-Type | Deflection-Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Accuracy | Very high | Moderate |
| Speed | Slower (requires balancing) | Fast (direct reading) |
| Sensitivity | High | Depends on instrument |
| Cost | Expensive | Economical |

---

**Part (b) — Error Analysis in Density (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 10.0$ cm, $\Delta L = 0.1$ cm
- $B = 5.0$ cm, $\Delta B = 0.1$ cm
- $H = 2.0$ cm, $\Delta H = 0.1$ cm
- $m = 850$ g, $\Delta m = 2$ g

**Step 1: Compute Volume (1 mark)**

$$V = L \times B \times H = 10.0 \times 5.0 \times 2.0 = 100.0 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Compute Density (1 mark)**

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{850 \text{ g}}{100.0 \text{ cm}^3} = 8.50 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Write the error propagation formula (1.5 marks)**

Since $\rho = m / (L \cdot B \cdot H)$, taking logarithms:

$$\ln \rho = \ln m - \ln L - \ln B - \ln H$$

Differentiating and taking absolute values:

$$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta B}{B} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta H}{H} \right\vert$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values (2 marks)**

$$\dfrac{\Delta m}{m} = \dfrac{2}{850} = 0.00235$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{0.1}{10.0} = 0.01000$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta B}{B} = \dfrac{0.1}{5.0} = 0.02000$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta H}{H} = \dfrac{0.1}{2.0} = 0.05000$$

**Step 5: Compute relative error (1 mark)**

$$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = 0.00235 + 0.01000 + 0.02000 + 0.05000 = 0.08235$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \approx 8.24\%$$

**Step 6: Compute absolute error (0.5 mark)**

$$\Delta \rho = \rho \times 0.08235 = 8.50 \times 0.08235 = 0.700 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Final Answer (0.5 mark):**

$$\boxed{\rho = 8.50 \pm 0.70 \text{ g/cm}^3 \quad \text{(Relative error} = 8.24\%)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT forget to take absolute values** when summing individual relative errors. Students often incorrectly add them with signs.
> - **Unit consistency** is critical. Mixing cm³ and m³ will cost 1 mark.
> - **The height $H$ has the largest relative error** (5%) despite having the smallest absolute error — emphasize that **relative error ≠ absolute error**.
> - The final answer **must** be reported in the form "value ± error," not just the error alone.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** Explain the **Indirect Method** of measurement with suitable examples. Derive the formula for the maximum relative error in a quantity $Y$ that is a function of multiple measurable quantities $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A resistance is measured using a **voltmeter and ammeter** as $V = 12.5 \pm 0.1$ V and $I = 2.50 \pm 0.05$ A. Compute **(i)** the resistance, **(ii)** the maximum absolute error in resistance, and **(iii)** the percentage error in power dissipated, where $P = V \times I$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Indirect Method and Error Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Indirect Method** of measurement determines the value of the measurand by measuring one or more *related* quantities and computing the desired value using a known **mathematical relationship** (formula). It is used when direct measurement is impractical, expensive, or impossible.

**Examples (2 marks):**
- **Density** of a solid: $\rho = m / V$, where mass $m$ and dimensions (for volume) are measured.
- **Electrical Resistance**: $R = V / I$, using Ohm's Law with measured voltage and current.
- **Strain**: $\epsilon = \Delta L / L$, where length change and original length are measured.
- **Temperature** using a **thermocouple**: EMF $E$ is measured and temperature $T$ is derived from the calibration table.

**Derivation of Maximum Relative Error (3 marks):**

Let the measurand be $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$, where each $X_i$ is independently measured.

Take the **natural logarithm** of both sides:

$$\ln Y = \ln f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to each $X_i$:

$$\dfrac{1}{Y} \cdot \dfrac{\partial Y}{\partial X_i} = \dfrac{1}{f} \cdot \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial X_i} = \dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial X_i}$$

For a **small change** $\Delta X_i$ in $X_i$, the change in $Y$ is:

$$\Delta Y_i = \dfrac{\partial Y}{\partial X_i} \cdot \Delta X_i$$

For the **worst case**, all errors add up (taking absolute values):

$$\Delta Y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{\partial Y}{\partial X_i} \right\vert \cdot \Delta X_i$$

Dividing both sides by $Y$:

$$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial X_i} \right\vert \cdot \Delta X_i$$

This is the **maximum relative error** in the indirectly measured quantity $Y$.

**Special Case (Power Form):** If $Y = K \cdot X_1^{a_1} \cdot X_2^{a_2} \cdots$, then:

$$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert a_i \vert \cdot \dfrac{\Delta X_i}{X_i}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem on Resistance and Power (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $V = 12.5$ V, $\Delta V = 0.1$ V
- $I = 2.50$ A, $\Delta I = 0.05$ A

**Step 1: Compute Resistance (1 mark)**

$$R = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{12.5}{2.50} = 5.00 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 2: Compute Relative Error in R (2 marks)**

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \right\vert = \dfrac{0.1}{12.5} + \dfrac{0.05}{2.50}$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = 0.008 + 0.020 = 0.028 = 2.8\%$$

**Step 3: Compute Absolute Error in R (0.5 mark)**

$$\Delta R = R \times 0.028 = 5.00 \times 0.028 = 0.14 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4: Report Final R (0.5 mark)**

$$\boxed{R = 5.00 \pm 0.14 \text{ }\Omega}$$

**Step 5: Compute Power (1 mark)**

$$P = V \times I = 12.5 \times 2.50 = 31.25 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Compute Relative Error in P (1.5 marks)**

Since $P = V \cdot I$ (product form, exponents both equal 1):

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} = \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \right\vert + \left\vert \dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \right\vert = 0.008 + 0.020 = 0.028 = 2.8\%$$

**Step 7: Compute Absolute Error in P (0.5 mark)**

$$\Delta P = P \times 0.028 = 31.25 \times 0.028 = 0.875 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{R = 5.00 \pm 0.14 \text{ }\Omega, \quad P = 31.25 \pm 0.875 \text{ W (2.8\% error)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Question B:**
> - Many students incorrectly write $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} - \dfrac{\Delta I}{I}$ (with subtraction) instead of **addition** (with absolute values). This is the most common error and costs **2–3 marks**.
> - The **power formula $P = VI$ has the same relative error form** as resistance because both are derived from $V$ and $I$ with linear exponents. Students often derive it incorrectly as a separate case.
> - Always state the **units** of the final answer ($\Omega$ for $R$, W for $P$). Missing units cost marks.
> - The problem says "maximum error" — you **must** sum absolute values, not use RMS or average.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Direct Method** = direct physical comparison with a standard; **no computation** needed (e.g., vernier, voltmeter, thermometer).
- **Indirect Method** = measure related quantities, then **compute** the desired value using a formula (e.g., density, resistance, strain).
- Direct methods are subdivided into **Null-type (zero deflection)** and **Deflection-type** methods.
- Null-type methods are **more accurate** because accuracy depends only on the standard, not on indicator linearity.
- Indirect methods suffer from **error accumulation** — the total error is the sum of weighted individual errors.
- The **error propagation formula** for $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ is:
  $$\Delta Y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial X_i} \right\vert \cdot \Delta X_i$$
- For the **power form** $Y = K \prod X_i^{a_i}$, relative error is:
  $$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \sum \vert a_i \vert \cdot \dfrac{\Delta X_i}{X_i}$$
- For a **sum or difference** $Y = X_1 \pm X_2$: absolute errors add directly: $\Delta Y = \Delta X_1 + \Delta X_2$.
- For a **product or quotient** $Y = X_1 \cdot X_2$ or $X_1 / X_2$: relative errors add directly: $\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{\Delta X_1}{X_1} + \dfrac{\Delta X_2}{X_2}$.
- Most modern **transducers (LVDT, strain gauge, RTD, thermocouple)** are examples of **Indirect Methods**.
- Always report final indirect results in the form $\boxed{Y = Y_0 \pm \Delta Y}$ with proper units.
- **Always use absolute values** when summing errors for worst-case analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT611 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Instruments" in Measurements?

In the context of the KTU 2024 OEMRT611 syllabus, the term **Instruments** under Module 3 refers to a class of **electrical and electronic measurement devices** used to quantify electrical quantities such as voltage, current, power, energy, frequency, and phase. These are the *secondary transducers* of the measurement chain — they convert an electrical signal into a readable analog or digital form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** An *instrument* is a device that uses a known physical principle (electromagnetic, electrostatic, thermal, or electronic) to produce a deflection, count, or display proportional to the measurand, with predictable accuracy, resolution, and linearity.

## 1.2 The Two Grand Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogue Instruments** — produce a continuous output that is a scaled replica of the measurand (e.g., pointer deflection).  
> **Digital Instruments** — convert the measurand into a discrete numerical readout using A/D conversion and logic circuits.

| Family | Examples | Reading Type | KTU Relevance |
|--------|----------|--------------|---------------|
| Electromechanical | PMMC, MI, Electrodynamometer, Induction | Pointer | Power, Energy |
| Electronic | CRO, DSO, Signal Generator, Q-Meter | Display | Waveform, Frequency |
| Digital | DVM, DMM, Digital Wattmeter | Numeric | Precision labs |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Measurement Engine"

Think of every instrument as a **three-stage car engine**:

- **Stage 1 (Deflector):** Receives the measurand current/voltage and converts it into a mechanical *force* (the "explosion" pushing the piston).
- **Stage 2 (Controller):** A spring (or its digital equivalent — a counter/ADC) opposes the force, providing a restoring action so the pointer settles.
- **Stage 3 (Damping):** Air vane or eddy-current damper prevents oscillation — like shock absorbers on a vehicle.

> [!TIP]
> **Universal truth in KTU exams:** Whenever an examiner asks "Why is the scale linear/non-linear?", the answer is *always* about the mathematical relationship between **deflecting torque** and the **measurand**.

## 1.4 Classification of Analogue Instruments by Operating Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Mandated Classification (must memorize for 2-mark questions):**

1. **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC)** — works on the *motor principle* (current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field). Only for **DC**.
2. **Moving Iron (MI)** — works on the *magnetic effect of current* (iron piece attracted to/repelled by a magnetized coil). For **AC & DC**.
3. **Electrodynamometer (EDD)** — works on the *interaction of two magnetic fields* produced by two coils. For **AC & DC** power.
4. **Induction Type** — works on the *induction principle* (eddy currents in an aluminum disc produced by alternating flux). For **AC energy**.
5. **Electrostatic** — works on the *Coulomb force* between charged plates. For high-voltage AC/DC.
6. **Rectifier Type** — combines a rectifier with a PMMC movement to measure AC quantities.

## 1.5 The Standard Instrument Block

```
[Measurand] → [Transducer/Sensor] → [Signal Conditioner] → [Deflection Mechanism] → [Pointer/Display] → [Reader]
```

Every KTU instrument fits into this pipeline. The "Instruments" topic in Module 3 focuses on the *deflection mechanism and display* stages, where the actual measurement is quantified.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Torque System (Universal Analogue Instrument Theory)

Every electromechanical pointer-type instrument has **three torques** acting on its moving system:

$$T_d \quad \text{(Deflecting torque — produced by the measurand)}$$
$$T_c \quad \text{(Controlling torque — produced by the spring, opposes } T_d \text{)}$$
$$T_d \quad \text{(Damping torque — opposes the *motion* of the pointer)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At steady state (final pointer position):** $T_d = T_c$ — this is the **equilibrium equation** from which every KTU instrument equation is derived.

## 2.2 PMMC (D'Arsonval) — Operating Principle

A rectangular coil of N turns is suspended in a **radial magnetic field** of flux density **B** (Weber/m²). When current **I** flows through the coil, a force is produced on each conductor of effective length **l**.

**Key relationships (memorize):**

$$F = BIl \quad \text{(force per conductor, Newtons)}$$

$$T_d = NBIA \quad \text{(total deflecting torque, where A is coil area)}$$

$$T_c = K_s \, \theta \quad \text{(spring controlling torque)}$$

$$\theta = \frac{NBA}{K_s} \, I \quad \text{(final pointer deflection in radians)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Scale is linear** in I because θ ∝ I. The PMMC has the highest accuracy among analog instruments — typically **±0.1 % to ±2 %** of full scale.

**Why a radial field?** Because B is always parallel to the coil sides, the force direction is constant irrespective of coil position. This eliminates the sine term that would otherwise make the scale non-linear.

## 2.3 Moving Iron (MI) — Attraction and Repulsion Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two sub-types: **Attraction type** (single iron pulled into a coil) and **Repulsion type** (two irons inside a coil, similarly magnetized, repel).

The energy stored in the magnetic field of the coil of inductance L carrying current I is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

The deflecting torque (derived by taking the partial derivative with respect to angular deflection θ):

$$T_d = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$$

For **DC**, $T_d \propto I^2$. For **AC** with instantaneous current $i = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, the average torque is:

$$T_{d,avg} = \frac{1}{2} I_{rms}^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{1}{...} $$

$$\boxed{T_{d,avg} = \frac{1}{2} I_{rms}^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Scale is SQUARE-LAW.** The pointer deflection is non-linear with I. To make the scale readable, the iron shape is *specially designed* (e.g., tapered vanes) to make dL/dθ vary inversely with θ, partially linearizing the scale at the upper end.

## 2.4 Electrodynamometer Wattmeter — The Most Exam-Favorite Instrument

Two coils:
- **Current coil (CC)** — low resistance, connected in series with the load.
- **Potential coil (PC)** — high resistance (in series with multiplier R), connected across the load.

Both coils carry currents proportional to the load current and load voltage respectively. The instantaneous torque is:

$$T_{d,inst} = K \, i_1 \, i_2$$

For DC: $T_d = K I_1 I_2$

For AC with $i_1 = I_{m1}\sin(\omega t)$ and $i_2 = I_{m2}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$:

$$T_{d,avg} = K I_{rms,1} I_{rms,2} \cos(\phi)$$

Since the potential coil current is $I_{PC} = V/Z_{PC} \approx V/R_{PC}$ (for non-inductive multiplier):

$$\boxed{\theta \propto V I \cos(\phi) = P_{real} \quad \text{(Real Power)}}$$

## 2.5 Single-Phase Induction-Type Energy Meter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **household electricity meter** — works on the *Ferraris principle*. Has two electromagnets: **Shunt magnet** (voltage-excited) and **Series magnet** (current-excited). Their alternating fluxes induce eddy currents in a thin aluminum disc, producing a driving torque.

**Driving torque (proportional to power):**
$$T_d = K_1 \, V I \cos(\phi) = K_1 P_{real}$$

**Braking torque (from a permanent magnet acting on the same disc):**
$$T_b = K_2 \, \omega = K_2 \cdot 2\pi N \quad \text{(N = disc speed in rps)}$$

**At steady state, $T_d = T_b$:**

$$K_1 V I \cos(\phi) = K_2 \omega$$

$$N = \frac{K_1}{2\pi K_2} \cdot P_{real} = K \cdot P \quad \text{(where K is the meter constant, rev/kWh)}$$

**Total energy in time t:**
$$E = \int_0^t P \, d\tau = \frac{N}{K} \quad \text{(kWh)}$$

## 2.6 Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) — The Workhorse

A CRO has these subsystems:
1. **CRT (Cathode Ray Tube)** — electron gun, deflection plates, phosphor screen.
2. **Vertical amplifier** — amplifies the input Y-signal.
3. **Horizontal amplifier** — drives the time-base (sawtooth) for X-axis.
4. **Time-base generator** — produces a linear ramp.
5. **Trigger circuit** — synchronizes the sweep with the input.
6. **Power supply** — high voltage (EHT) for CRT.

**Deflection equation (Y-axis):**

$$y = \frac{L \cdot D}{2 \, V_d} \cdot V_y = S_y \cdot V_y$$

Where:
- $L$ = distance from deflection plates to screen (m)
- $D$ = separation between Y-plates (m)
- $V_d$ = deflecting plate voltage (V)
- $V_y$ = input signal voltage (V)
- $S_y$ = deflection sensitivity (m/V or cm/V)

**Amplitude measurement from screen:**
$$V_{pp} = \frac{\text{peak-to-peak vertical divisions} \times \text{V/div}}{2}$$

## 2.7 AC Bridges — The General Balance Condition

Any four-arm AC bridge obeys the **universal balance condition** at null deflection of the detector:

$$\boxed{Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3 \quad \text{(magnitude and phase, i.e., complex balance)}}$$

In rectangular form: the **sum of phase angles** of opposite arms must be equal:
$$\angle Z_1 + \angle Z_4 = \angle Z_2 + \angle Z_3$$

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print and paste this table on your wall. Every formula here is a guaranteed KTU question.**

| # | Instrument / Concept | Governing Equation | Notes |
|---|---------------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | PMMC deflection | $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K_s} I$ | Linear scale, DC only |
| 2 | Moving Iron torque | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Square law scale |
| 3 | EDD Wattmeter reading | $P = V I \cos(\phi) - \text{error terms}$ | AC & DC power |
| 4 | EDD Wattmeter error | $R \cdot \tan(\phi) \cdot \tan(\theta) \cdot P_{true}$ | Phase-angle error |
| 5 | Energy meter speed | $N = K \cdot P$ | K = meter constant (rev/kWh) |
| 6 | Energy meter disc revs | $N = E / K$ | E in kWh |
| 7 | CRO Y-deflection | $y = \dfrac{L D}{2 V_d} V_y$ | Sensitivity $S_y = y/V_y$ |
| 8 | CRO Lissajous phase | $\sin(\phi) = \dfrac{y_1}{y_2}$ | For 1:1 frequency ratio |
| 9 | Bridge balance | $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ | Complex equality |
| 10 | Maxwell bridge L₁ | $L_1 = R_2 R_3 C_4$ | For medium-Q coils (Q: 1–10) |
| 11 | Maxwell bridge R₁ | $R_1 = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}$ | Series resistance |
| 12 | Hay bridge L₁ | $L_1 = \dfrac{R_2 R_3 C_4}{1 + (\omega R_4 C_4)^2}$ | For high-Q coils (Q > 10) |
| 13 | Wien bridge freq. | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_3 C_1 C_2}}$ | Used in oscillators |
| 14 | Schering bridge C₁ | $C_1 = C_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$ | For capacitance, D-factor |
| 15 | Anderson bridge L₁ | $L_1 = R_2 R_3 C_4 \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} + \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}\right)$ | For precise inductance |
| 16 | Q-meter Q | $Q = \dfrac{\omega L}{R} = \dfrac{V_C}{V_{in}}$ | V_c across test coil |
| 17 | Two-wattmeter P | $P = P_1 + P_2$ | 3-phase, 2-wattmeter method |
| 18 | Two-wattmeter PF | $\tan(\phi) = \sqrt{3}\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2}$ | 3-phase balanced |
| 19 | 3-phase reactive | $Q = \sqrt{3}(P_1 - P_2)$ | VAR, 2-wattmeter method |
| 20 | DVM resolution | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{1}{10^n - 1}$ | n = number of digits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Watch (KTU frequently tests this):**
> - $N$ = number of turns
> - $B$ = Tesla (T) or Weber/m²
> - $I$ = Ampere
> - $A$ = m² (coil area)
> - $K_s$ = N·m/rad (spring constant)
> - $\theta$ = radians (pointer deflection)
> - $L$ = Henry (H)
> - $C$ = Farad (F)
> - $\omega$ = rad/s
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deflecting Torque of a PMMC Instrument

**Given:** A rectangular coil of N turns, carrying current I, immersed in a uniform radial magnetic field B.

**Step 1 — Force on one side of the coil:**
A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences the Lorentz force. For a single straight wire of length $l$ in a radial field:

$$F = BIl$$

**Step 2 — Total force considering all conductors:**
The coil has **two effective sides** (top and bottom) where the force is vertical. With N turns stacked together, the total number of effective conductors is $2N$:

$$F_{total} = BIl \times 2N = 2NBIl$$

**Step 3 — Convert force to torque:**
The force acts at the perpendicular distance equal to the **half-width** of the coil, $b/2$, on each side. The total torque arm from the pivot axis is therefore $b$ (the coil width). Alternatively, the moment arm equals the radius $r$ from the coil center to the edge.

Using the coil's effective area $A = l \times b$ (length × width):

$$T_d = F_{total} \times \frac{b}{2} = (BIl) \times N \times b = NBIlb = NBA \cdot I$$

(We combined the two sides by writing the full coil area $A$ instead of $2 \times l \times b/2 = lb$.)

**Step 4 — Spring controlling torque:**
A phosphor-bronze hair spring provides a restoring torque proportional to the angular deflection $\theta$:

$$T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium (steady state):**
At final pointer position, the net torque is zero:

$$T_d = T_c$$

$$NBA \cdot I = K_s \cdot \theta$$

**Step 6 — Final deflection equation:**

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{NBA}{K_s} \cdot I}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The constant of proportionality $\dfrac{NBA}{K_s}$** is called the **figure of merit** of the PMMC. A high value means high sensitivity (large pointer movement for small current).

**Why is this the most asked derivation?** Because it covers Newton's law, Lorentz force, torque, Hooke's law (spring), and equilibrium — all in one. KTU loves it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deflecting Torque of a Moving-Iron Instrument

**Given:** A coil of self-inductance $L(\theta)$ — inductance varies with angular position of the iron. Current $i(t)$ flows.

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the magnetic field:**
When the current is $i$ and the inductance is $L(\theta)$:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2 \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

**Step 2 — Principle of virtual work:**
The deflecting torque equals the rate of change of stored energy with respect to angular position, holding current constant (since the coil is supplied by a current source, not a voltage source, in this derivation framework):

$$T_d = \frac{\partial W}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — For DC (constant current I):**
Direct substitution:

$$T_d = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$$

**Step 4 — For AC, instantaneous current $i = I_m \sin(\omega t)$:**
Instantaneous torque:

$$T_{d,inst} = \frac{1}{2} I_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \frac{dL}{d\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Average over one cycle:**
Using $\langle \sin^2(\omega t) \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2}$:

$$T_{d,avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{I_m^2}{2} \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2} I_{rms}^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$$

$$\boxed{T_{d,avg} = \frac{1}{2} I_{rms}^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of dL/dθ:** This is the *change in coil inductance as the iron moves.* If the iron shape is tapered cleverly, dL/dθ can be made to vary with θ so the scale becomes approximately linear at the upper half. This is why the lower 10–20 % of an MI scale is compressed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maxwell's Inductance Bridge — Balance Equations

**Given:** Standard Maxwell's bridge, with arms $Z_1$ (unknown inductance $L_1$ in series with resistance $R_1$), $Z_2 = R_2$, $Z_3 = R_3$, $Z_4 = R_4$ in parallel with a calibrated capacitor $C_4$.

**Step 1 — Write the complex impedances:**

$$Z_1 = R_1 + j\omega L_1 \quad \text{(unknown)}$$

$$Z_2 = R_2 \quad \text{(pure resistance)}$$

$$Z_3 = R_3 \quad \text{(pure resistance)}$$

$$Z_4 = \frac{R_4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}}{R_4 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4}} = \frac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the universal balance condition $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$:**

$$(R_1 + j\omega L_1) \cdot \frac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $(1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)$:**

$$(R_1 + j\omega L_1) \cdot R_4 = R_2 R_3 (1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)$$

$$R_1 R_4 + j\omega L_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3 + j\omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4$$

**Step 4 — Equate real parts:**

$$R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$$

$$\boxed{R_1 = \frac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}}$$

**Step 5 — Equate imaginary parts:**

$$\omega L_1 R_4 = \omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4$$

Canceling $\omega R_4$ (both sides positive):

$$\boxed{L_1 = R_2 R_3 C_4}$$

**Step 6 — Quality factor of the unknown coil:**

$$Q = \frac{\omega L_1}{R_1} = \omega R_4 C_4$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why is Maxwell bridge preferred for low-Q coils?** Because the $Q$ of the unknown appears only as $\omega R_4 C_4$, which can be set to a low value easily. The balance equations are **independent of frequency** for $L_1$ and $R_1$ — this is the bridge's biggest advantage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hay Bridge — Balance Equations

**Given:** Hay bridge — arm 4 has a resistor $R_4$ in **series** with a capacitor $C_4$ (this is what makes it different from Maxwell).

**Step 1 — Impedances:**

$$Z_1 = R_1 + j\omega L_1 \quad \text{(unknown)}$$

$$Z_2 = R_2 \quad \text{(resistor)}$$

$$Z_3 = R_3 \quad \text{(resistor)}$$

$$Z_4 = R_4 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_4} = R_4 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_4}$$

**Step 2 — Balance condition $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$:**

$$(R_1 + j\omega L_1)\left(R_4 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_4}\right) = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 3 — Expand the left-hand side:**

$$R_1 R_4 - \dfrac{j R_1}{\omega C_4} + j\omega L_1 R_4 - j^2 \dfrac{\omega L_1}{\omega C_4}$$

$$= R_1 R_4 + \dfrac{L_1}{C_4} + j\left(\omega L_1 R_4 - \dfrac{R_1}{\omega C_4}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Equate real parts:**

$$R_1 R_4 + \dfrac{L_1}{C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 5 — Equate imaginary parts:**

$$\omega L_1 R_4 - \dfrac{R_1}{\omega C_4} = 0$$

$$\omega^2 L_1 R_4 C_4 = R_1$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $R_1 = \omega^2 L_1 R_4 C_4$ into the real equation:**

$$(\omega^2 L_1 R_4 C_4) R_4 + \dfrac{L_1}{C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

$$L_1 \left(\omega^2 R_4^2 C_4 + \dfrac{1}{C_4}\right) = R_2 R_3$$

$$L_1 \cdot \dfrac{\omega^2 R_4^2 C_4^2 + 1}{C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 7 — Final balance equation for $L_1$:**

$$\boxed{L_1 = \dfrac{R_2 R_3 C_4}{1 + (\omega R_4 C_4)^2}}$$

**Step 8 — And the resistance $R_1$:**

$$\boxed{R_1 = \dfrac{\omega^2 L_1 R_4^2 C_4}{1} = \omega^2 L_1 C_4 R_4^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} \text{ (when substituted into the imaginary part)}}$$

Or more cleanly: $R_1 = \omega^2 L_1 C_4 R_4^2 / 1$ becomes $R_1 = \omega^2 L_1 R_4^2 C_4 / 1$ — let me redo:

$$R_1 = \omega^2 L_1 R_4 C_4$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hay bridge contains frequency $\omega$ in its balance equation** — meaning the bridge must be re-balanced if the supply frequency drifts. Use it for **high-Q coils** (Q > 10) and measure at a known, stable frequency.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Single-Phase Induction-Type Energy Meter — Speed Equation

**Step 1 — Driving torque from interaction of two fluxes:**
The series-magnet flux $\phi_s \propto I_{load}$ and the shunt-magnet flux $\phi_p \propto V_{supply}$. They produce a driving torque on the aluminum disc proportional to the product of fluxes and the phase angle between them:

$$T_d = K_1 \phi_p \phi_s \sin(\alpha) = K_1' V I \cos(\phi) = K_1' P_{real}$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between $\phi_p$ and $\phi_s$, designed to be $90° - \phi$ to yield $\cos(\phi)$ directly.

**Step 2 — Braking torque from the permanent magnet:**
A permanent magnet produces a steady flux $\phi_b$ linking the disc. The disc rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ in this flux, inducing an EMF, an eddy current, and a reaction torque:

$$T_b = K_2 \phi_b^2 \omega = K_2' \omega = K_2' (2\pi N)$$

where $N$ is the disc speed in revolutions per second (rps).

**Step 3 — Steady-state equilibrium ($T_d = T_b$):**

$$K_1' P_{real} = K_2' (2\pi N)$$

$$N = \dfrac{K_1'}{2\pi K_2'} P_{real} = K P_{real}$$

$$\boxed{N = K P \quad \text{(rev/s = constant × power)}}$$

**Step 4 — Total energy in time $t$ seconds:**

$$E = P_{real} \cdot t = \dfrac{N \cdot t}{K} \quad \text{(Watt-seconds)}$$

Converting to kWh (1 kWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ W·s):

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{N \cdot t}{K} \quad \text{kWh, where K is in rev/kWh}}$$

**Step 5 — Practical energy reading from the disc revs:**

$$E = \dfrac{N_{observed}}{K_{rated}} \quad \text{kWh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Mixing up units. The meter constant $K$ is almost always expressed in **rev/kWh**, not rev/W·s. Always convert.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Two-Wattmeter Method for 3-Phase Power

**Given:** A balanced 3-phase load with line voltage $V_L$ and line current $I_L$, with phase angle $\phi$ between phase voltage and phase current.

**Step 1 — Wattmeter W₁ reads:**
W₁ is connected with its current coil in line R and its voltage coil between R and Y:

$$P_1 = V_{L} I_{L} \cos(30° - \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Wattmeter W₂ reads:**
W₂ is connected with its current coil in line B and its voltage coil between B and Y:

$$P_2 = V_{L} I_{L} \cos(30° + \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Total real power:**

$$\boxed{P = P_1 + P_2 = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos(\phi)}$$

**Step 4 — Power factor derivation:**
$$P_1 + P_2 = V_L I_L \left[\cos(30° - \phi) + \cos(30° + \phi)\right] = V_L I_L \cdot 2\cos(30°)\cos(\phi) = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos(\phi)$$

$$P_1 - P_2 = V_L I_L \left[\cos(30° - \phi) - \cos(30° + \phi)\right] = V_L I_L \cdot 2\sin(30°)\sin(\phi) = V_L I_L \sin(\phi)$$

**Step 5 — Phase angle and PF:**

$$\tan(\phi) = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2}$$

$$\boxed{\cos(\phi) = \cos\left[\tan^{-1}\left(\sqrt{3}\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 + P_2}\right)\right]}$$

**Step 6 — Reactive power:**

$$\boxed{Q = \sqrt{3}(P_1 - P_2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam tip:** When asked to find the power factor for a 3-phase load, always use the two-wattmeter method if the neutral is not accessible. The above formula is *the* shortcut.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A PMMC instrument has $N = 100$ turns, coil area $A = 4 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$, magnetic flux density $B = 0.1 \, T$, and spring constant $K_s = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, N \cdot m / rad$. Find the current required to produce a deflection of $60°$ ($\pi/3$ rad).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the equilibrium equation:

$$NBA \cdot I = K_s \theta$$

**Step 2:** Solve for I:

$$I = \dfrac{K_s \theta}{NBA}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values:

$$I = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(\pi/3)}{(100)(0.1)(4 \times 10^{-4})}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator: $5 \times 10^{-6} \times \pi/3 = 5.236 \times 10^{-6}$

**Step 5:** Compute denominator: $100 \times 0.1 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 4 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 6:** Divide:

$$I = \dfrac{5.236 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.309 \times 10^{-3} \, A = 1.309 \, mA$$

**Final Answer:** $I \approx 1.31 \, mA$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Functional Architecture of a Pointer-Type Instrument

This diagram maps the **universal flow** of an analog indicating instrument — every instrument from PMMC to wattmeter conforms to this block architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measurand Source<br/>Voltage or Current] --> B[Input Attenuator<br/>Multiplier Resistor or Shunt]
    B --> C[Deflection Mechanism<br/>PMMC, MI, EDD, Induction]
    C --> D[Deflecting Torque Td<br/>Proportional to Measurand]
    D --> E{Moving System<br/>Pointer + Coil}
    E --> F[Controlling Spring<br/>Restoring Torque Tc]
    F --> G{Torque Equilibrium<br/>Td equals Tc}
    G --> H[Damping Subsystem<br/>Air Vane or Eddy Current]
    H --> I[Pointer Display<br/>Calibrated Scale]
    I --> J[Observer<br/>Reads Value]
    B --> K[Sensor Protection<br/>Fuse and Surge Divertor]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 CRO Functional Block Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

The Cathode Ray Oscilloscope has six cooperating subsystems. The following Mermaid block diagram shows their signal-flow interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Y Input<br/>Measurand Signal] --> VA[Vertical Amplifier<br/>Gain and Level Shift]
    VA --> VDELAY[Vertical Delay Line]
    VDELAY --> VPLATE[Vertical Deflection Plates<br/>Y Axis in CRT]
    TBG[Time Base Generator<br/>Sawtooth Ramp] --> HA[Horizontal Amplifier]
    HA --> HPLATE[Horizontal Deflection Plates<br/>X Axis in CRT]
    TRG[Trigger Circuit<br/>Sync to Input] --> TBG
    VA -.sync.-> TRG
    CRT[Cathode Ray Tube<br/>Electron Gun and Phosphor Screen] -.contains.-> VPLATE
    CRT -.contains.-> HPLATE
    PS[Power Supply<br/>EHT 10kV to 25kV] --> CRT
    ZIN[Z Modulation Input<br/>Intensity Control] --> CRT
```

## 4.3 AC Bridge — Maxwell's Inductance Bridge Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[AC Source<br/>1 kHz to 10 kHz] --> NODE1((Node A))
    NODE1 --> Z1[Arm Z1<br/>Unknown Inductance<br/>L1 plus R1]
    NODE1 --> Z2[Arm Z2<br/>Known Resistor R2]
    Z1 --> NODE2((Node B))
    Z2 --> NODE2
    NODE2 --> Z3[Arm Z3<br/>Known Resistor R3]
    NODE2 --> Z4[Arm Z4<br/>R4 in Parallel with C4]
    Z3 --> NODE3((Node C))
    Z4 --> NODE3
    NODE3 --> GND[Ground Node D]
    NODE2 --> DET[Detector<br/>Headphones or VTVM]
    DET --> NODE3
    DET -.null at balance.-> NULL[Null Indicator]
```

## 4.4 Single-Phase Induction Energy Meter — Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LINESUP[AC Mains<br/>L and N] --> SM[Series Magnet Coil<br/>Current Coil]
    SM --> LOAD[Connected Load]
    LINESUP --> PM[Shunt Magnet Coil<br/>Pressure Coil]
    PM --> LOAD
    SM -.magnetic flux.-> DISC[Aluminum Disc<br/>Thin Eddy Current Rotor]
    PM -.magnetic flux.-> DISC
    DISC -.drives.-> SPINDLE[Spindle and Gear Train]
    SPINDLE --> REG[Register Dials<br/>kWh Counter]
    PMM[Permanent Magnet<br/>Braking System] -.magnetic flux.-> DISC
    DISC -.rotation in PM flux.-> EDDY[Eddy Current Braking Torque]
```

## 4.5 Q-Meter Block Diagram

The Q-meter uses a calibrated variable capacitor and a series resonance principle to measure the quality factor of coils and capacitors.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OSC[Oscillator<br/>Variable Frequency Source] --> RHEO[Series Resistance<br/>Rsh Small MOhms]
    RHEO --> TEST[Test Coil<br/>Lt and Rt Unknown]
    TEST --> VARC[Variable Calibrated<br/>Capacitor Cvar]
    VARC --> GND[Ground Return]
    VMETER[Voltmeter Vx<br/>Across Test Coil] -.reads.-> VXCALC
    VIN[Voltmeter Vin<br/>Across Oscillator] -.reads.-> VINCALC
    VXCALC --> CALC[Compute Q equals Vx divided by Vin]
    VINCALC --> CALC
    CALC --> DISP[Q Factor Display]
```

## 4.6 Two-Wattmeter Method for 3-Phase Power

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Line R] --> W1CC[W1 Current Coil]
    Y[Line Y] --> W2V[W2 Voltage Coil]
    B[Line B] --> W2CC[W2 Current Coil]
    W1CC --> LOAD[Three Phase Load]
    W2CC --> LOAD
    R --> W1V[W1 Voltage Coil]
    W1V --> Y
    W1V -.reads.-> P1[P1 Value]
    W2V -.reads.-> P2[P2 Value]
    P1 --> SUM[Total Power equals P1 plus P2]
    P2 --> SUM
    SUM --> PF[Power Factor Calculation]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between PMMC and Moving Iron instruments with respect to working principle, scale type, and application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PMMC (D'Arsonval) | Moving Iron |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Working principle | Current-carrying coil in a permanent magnetic field (motor principle) | Soft iron piece attracted into a magnetized coil (magnetic effect of current) |
| Scale type | Linear (θ ∝ I) | Non-linear / square law (θ ∝ I²) |
| Measurand | DC only | AC and DC |
| Sensitivity | High | Moderate |
| Accuracy | Highest (0.1–2 %) | Moderate (2–5 %) |
| Eddy current error | None | Present in AC |
| Construction | Delicate, costly | Rugged, cheaper |

PMMC uses a moving coil in a fixed magnetic field, while MI uses a fixed coil with a moving iron piece. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Why is the energy meter called a "time-integrating wattmeter"? Explain with the speed equation.**

**Model Answer:**

An induction-type energy meter has its disc rotation speed $N$ (revolutions per second) directly proportional to the real power being consumed. This is shown by the steady-state torque balance:

$$N = K \cdot P_{real}$$

The number of revolutions $N$ over a time interval $t$ therefore represents the *time integral* of power:

$$N \cdot t = K \int_0^t P \, d\tau = K \cdot E_{energy}$$

By counting the total revolutions through a gear train onto a register dial, the meter directly *integrates* the power over time to give total energy. Hence the name **"time-integrating wattmeter"** or **"watt-hour meter"**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Part B questions of 14 marks carry an **internal choice**. You must answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B. Each choice has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the equation for the deflecting torque of a Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument. Show that the scale is linear. List the merits and demerits of PMMC.

(b) **[7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a single-phase induction-type energy meter. Derive the equation for the disc speed in terms of power and the meter constant.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Construction of PMMC:**
A PMMC instrument has a permanent magnet (Alnico or Alcomax) shaped to produce a radial magnetic field across a cylindrical iron core. A light rectangular coil of fine copper wire wound on an aluminum former is pivoted (jewelled bearings) so it can rotate in the air gap. A phosphor-bronze hair spring provides the controlling torque and also serves as one current lead. A pointer attached to the coil moves over a calibrated scale. Eddy-current damping is provided by the aluminum former moving in the magnetic field.

**Derivation of deflecting torque:**

Consider a single conductor of effective length $l$ carrying current $I$ in a radial magnetic field $B$.

Force on one conductor: $F = BIl$

For a coil with $N$ turns and two effective sides: $F_{total} = 2N BIl$

If the coil width is $b$, the torque arm for one side is $b/2$. Total torque from both sides:

$$T_d = 2 \times NBIl \times \frac{b}{2} = NBIlb = NBIA$$

where $A = l \times b$ is the coil area.

**Controlling torque from the spring:** $T_c = K_s \theta$

**At equilibrium:** $T_d = T_c \Rightarrow NBA \cdot I = K_s \theta$

$$\theta = \frac{NBA}{K_s} \cdot I$$

Since $NBA/K_s$ is a constant, $\theta \propto I$ — therefore the **scale is linear**.

**Merits of PMMC:**
1. High accuracy (0.1 % to 2 % of full scale).
2. Linear scale, easy to read.
3. Low power consumption.
4. Good damping (eddy current in aluminum former).
5. No hysteresis loss (permanent magnet is stationary).

**Demerits of PMMC:**
1. Suitable **only for DC** (alternating current would average to zero and could even demagnetize the magnet).
2. Costlier than MI.
3. Delicate construction (jewel bearings).
4. Errors due to aging of the permanent magnet (re-magnetization needed over time).
5. Temperature effects on the spring.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Construction of single-phase induction energy meter:**

The meter has:
- **Current (series) electromagnet** — a few turns of thick wire connected in series with the load. It produces a flux $\phi_s \propto I_{load}$.
- **Pressure (shunt) electromagnet** — many turns of fine wire connected across the supply. It produces a flux $\phi_p \propto V_{supply}$.
- **Aluminum (or copper) disc** — a thin non-magnetic disc mounted on a vertical spindle, free to rotate between the two electromagnet poles.
- **Permanent braking magnet** — a small strong permanent magnet, positioned so that the disc rotates through its air gap.
- **Register (counting mechanism)** — a gear train from the spindle drives calibrated dials that display kWh.
- **Phase-compensating band/shading ring** — a small copper ring on the shunt magnet limb to adjust the phase between $\phi_p$ and $\phi_s$ so that the meter reads correctly on inductive loads.

**Working principle:**
The interaction of the alternating fluxes $\phi_p$ and $\phi_s$ (which are not in phase with each other) induces eddy currents in the aluminum disc. The interaction between the eddy currents and the respective fluxes produces a **driving torque** on the disc in a direction given by Lenz's law. The disc therefore rotates at a steady speed.

**Derivation of disc speed:**

Driving torque is proportional to the real power:
$$T_d = K_1 V I \cos(\phi) = K_1 P$$

Braking torque from the permanent magnet (proportional to disc speed $\omega$):
$$T_b = K_2 \omega = K_2 (2\pi N)$$

At steady state, $T_d = T_b$:
$$K_1 P = 2\pi K_2 N$$
$$N = \frac{K_1}{2\pi K_2} P = K P$$

So the disc speed (in rev/s) is directly proportional to the real power. The total revolutions in time $t$ give the energy in kWh:
$$E = \frac{N \cdot t}{K} \quad \text{kWh (when K is rev/kWh)}$$

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With a circuit diagram, derive the balance equations for a Maxwell's inductance-capacitance bridge. Show that the bridge is suitable for measuring the inductance of medium-Q coils.

(b) **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional subsystems of a Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO). Derive the expression for the vertical deflection of the electron beam in terms of the input signal voltage.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Maxwell Bridge Diagram (text description):**
- AC source connected between nodes A and D.
- Arm 1 (between A and B): unknown inductance $L_1$ in series with resistance $R_1$.
- Arm 2 (between A and C): known non-inductive resistance $R_2$.
- Arm 3 (between C and D): known non-inductive resistance $R_3$.
- Arm 4 (between B and D): known non-inductive resistance $R_4$ in **parallel** with a calibrated standard capacitor $C_4$.
- Detector (headphones or VTVM) between B and C.

**Derivation:**

The complex balance condition is $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$.

$Z_1 = R_1 + j\omega L_1$

$Z_4 = \dfrac{R_4 \cdot (1/j\omega C_4)}{R_4 + 1/j\omega C_4} = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}$

Substituting:

$$(R_1 + j\omega L_1) \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

Multiply both sides by $(1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)$:

$$(R_1 + j\omega L_1) R_4 = R_2 R_3 (1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)$$

$$R_1 R_4 + j\omega L_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3 + j\omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4$$

Equating real parts:
$$R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3 \Rightarrow \boxed{R_1 = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}}$$

Equating imaginary parts:
$$\omega L_1 R_4 = \omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4 \Rightarrow \boxed{L_1 = R_2 R_3 C_4}$$

**Q-factor:** $Q = \dfrac{\omega L_1}{R_1} = \omega R_4 C_4$

**Suitability for medium-Q coils:** The expressions for $R_1$ and $L_1$ are **independent of frequency** $\omega$. The Q-factor appears only as $\omega R_4 C_4$ which can be set to small values (1 to 10) by choosing $R_4$ and $C_4$ appropriately. This makes the bridge especially suitable for **medium-Q coils (Q: 1 to 10)**, since accurate balance can be achieved without large R or C values.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Block diagram (functional subsystems of a CRO):**
1. **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)** — electron gun (cathode, grid, anodes) producing a focused electron beam; Y-deflection plates; X-deflection plates; phosphor screen.
2. **Vertical amplifier** — amplifies the Y-input signal to a level sufficient to drive the Y-plates.
3. **Horizontal amplifier** — amplifies the time-base (sawtooth) signal to drive the X-plates.
4. **Time-base (sweep) generator** — produces a linear sawtooth ramp that moves the beam across the screen.
5. **Trigger circuit** — synchronizes the sweep generator to the input signal.
6. **Power supply** — EHT (10 kV to 25 kV) for the CRT, plus low-voltage supplies for amplifiers.

**Derivation of vertical deflection:**

Consider a single electron of charge $e$ and mass $m_e$ entering the Y-deflection plates with axial velocity $v_x$. The plates have length $L$, separation $D$, and the deflecting potential difference $V_d$.

**Step 1:** Electric field between plates:
$$E_y = \dfrac{V_d}{D}$$

**Step 2:** Transverse force on the electron:
$$F_y = e E_y = \dfrac{e V_d}{D}$$

**Step 3:** Transverse acceleration:
$$a_y = \dfrac{F_y}{m_e} = \dfrac{e V_d}{m_e D}$$

**Step 4:** Time the electron spends between the plates:
$$t = \dfrac{L}{v_x}$$

**Step 5:** Transverse velocity acquired:
$$v_y = a_y \cdot t = \dfrac{e V_d L}{m_e D v_x}$$

**Step 6:** Transverse displacement at the exit of the plates:
$$y_1 = \frac{1}{2} a_y t^2 = \dfrac{e V_d L^2}{2 m_e D v_x^2}$$

**Step 7:** After exiting the plates, the beam travels a further distance $L_1$ to the screen. By similar triangles, the additional deflection is:
$$y_2 = y_1 \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L/2} = y_1 \cdot \dfrac{2 L_1}{L}$$

**Step 8:** Total deflection at the screen (combining $y_1$ and $y_2$):
