**Interpretation:** Each 1°C temperature change produces 500 V output — extremely useful for precise temperature control loops.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linearity Error Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A pressure gauge has a nominal range of 0–10 bar. The actual output is fitted to a straight line, and the maximum deviation from the ideal line is found to be 0.08 bar. Calculate the linearity error in % of full scale (FS).

### Given:
- Range: $x_{max} = 10 \text{ bar}$
- Maximum deviation: $\Delta y_{max} = 0.08 \text{ bar}$

### Formula:

$$
\% \text{Linearity Error} = \frac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\%
$$

### Step-by-Step Calculation:

**Step 1:** Identify the full-scale output $y_{FS}$.

For a linear system: $y_{FS} = K \cdot x_{max} = K \cdot 10 \text{ bar}$

**Step 2:** Express the linearity error in terms of input equivalent (or output equivalent — both yield same percentage).

$$
\% \text{Linearity Error} = \frac{0.08}{10} \times 100\% = 0.8\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always express linearity error as a **percentage of full scale (FS)**, not as a percentage of the actual reading. The latter would give a "reading error" which is different and often smaller.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Static vs Dynamic Characteristics

| Characteristic | Static | Dynamic | KTU Frequency |
|----------------|--------|---------|---------------|
| **Sensitivity (K)** | ✓ | — | Very High |
| **Range & Span** | ✓ | — | High |
| **Linearity** | ✓ | — | Very High |
| **Hysteresis** | ✓ | — | High |
| **Dead Zone** | ✓ | — | Medium |
| **Resolution** | ✓ | — | High |
| **Drift** | ✓ | — | Medium |
| **Time Constant ($\tau$)** | — | ✓ | Very High |
| **Damping Ratio ($\zeta$)** | — | ✓ | Very High |
| **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$)** | — | ✓ | High |
| **Rise Time ($t_r$)** | — | ✓ | High |
| **Settling Time ($t_s$)** | — | ✓ | High |
| **Overshoot ($M_p$)** | — | ✓ | High |
| **Bandwidth (BW)** | — | ✓ | Medium |
| **Phase Shift ($\phi$)** | — | ✓ | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Element Generalized Measurement System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MEASURAND\nPhysical Quantity] --> B[1. PRIMARY SENSING\nELEMENT\nDetector]
    B --> C[2. VARIABLE\nCONVERSION ELEMENT\nDomain Converter]
    C --> D[3. VARIABLE\nMANIPULATION ELEMENT\nSignal Conditioning]
    D --> E[4. DATA TRANSMISSION\nELEMENT\nSignal Carrier]
    E --> F[5. DATA PRESENTATION\nELEMENT\nDisplay or Recorder]
    F --> G[6. DATA STORAGE OR\nFEEDBACK ELEMENT\nMemory or Controller]
    G --> H[OBSERVER\nor ACTUATOR]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Three-Stage Simplified Model (Doebelin)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[INPUT STAGE\nSensor] --> Q[INTERMEDIATE STAGE\nSignal Conditioning] --> R[OUTPUT STAGE\nPresentation]

    P -.->|Transduction| P
    Q -.->|Amplification Filter Linearization| Q
    R -.->|Display Recording Control| R

    style P fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style R fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Feedback Measurement System (Closed Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[M] --> SUM[+]
    SUM --> A1[Forward Path\nK_Forward]
    A1 --> Y[Output y]
    Y --> B1[Feedback Path\nBeta]
    B1 --> SUM
    SUM -->|Error| A1

    style X fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Y fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style SUM fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{K_{forward}}{1 + K_{forward} \cdot \beta}
$$

When $K_{forward} \cdot \beta \gg 1$:

$$
T(s) \approx \frac{1}{\beta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that in a closed-loop system, the overall sensitivity depends **only on $\beta$** (the feedback element), not on the forward path. This is why **self-balancing potentiometers, servo accelerometers, and force-balance pressure sensors** achieve high accuracy and linearity.

## 4.4 Application Domain Map of Measurement Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MS[Measurement Systems] --> IND[Industrial]
    MS --> SCI[Scientific Research]
    MS --> MED[Medical Biomedical]
    MS --> AERO[Automotive Aerospace]
    MS --> ENV[Environmental]
    MS --> CONS[Consumer Electronics]

    IND --> IND1[Process Control]
    IND --> IND2[Manufacturing QA]
    SCI --> SCI1[Particle Physics]
    SCI --> SCI2[Material Testing]
    MED --> MED1[Patient Monitoring]
    MED --> MED2[Diagnostic Imaging]
    AERO --> AERO1[Engine Control ECU]
    AERO --> AERO2[Aircraft Instrumentation]
    ENV --> ENV1[Weather Stations]
    ENV --> ENV2[Pollution Monitoring]
    CONS --> CONS1[Smartphones]
    CONS --> CONS2[Smart Home IoT]

    style MS fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style IND fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style SCI fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style MED fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style AERO fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style ENV fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style CONS fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.5 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OL[Open Loop System]
        OL1[Input x] --> OL2[Instrument K] --> OL3[Output y]
    end

    subgraph CL[Closed Loop System]
        CL1[Input x] --> CLadd[Summing Junction +] --> CL2[Forward K_f] --> CL4[Output y]
        CL4 --> CL3[Feedback Beta] --> CLadd
    end

    style OL1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style OL2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OL3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style CL1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style CL2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style CL4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style CL3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

| Feature | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Accuracy** | Limited by component tolerances | Depends on feedback element only |
| **Linearity** | Depends on every element | Intrinsically linear (inverting amplifier behavior) |
| **Sensitivity to noise** | High | Reduced (common-mode rejection) |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Low | Higher |
| **Examples** | Analog voltmeter, mercury thermometer | Servo accelerometer, force-balance sensor |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Measurement System. List its primary functions. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Measurement System** is an assembly of instruments and components arranged to sense, condition, transmit, and present the value of a physical quantity (measurand) in a meaningful, standardized form.

The **three primary functions** of a measurement system are:

1. **Indicating** — Displaying the current value of the measurand.
2. **Recording** — Capturing the value over time for archival analysis.
3. **Controlling** — Using the measured value to regulate a process automatically.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing & explaining 3 functions)*

### Question A2: What is a "Primary Sensing Element"? Give two examples. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Primary Sensing Element** is the first functional block in a measurement system that comes into direct contact with the measurand and produces an output signal proportional to it. It performs the initial energy conversion from the measurand domain to a more usable domain.

**Examples (any two):**
- **Thermocouple** — converts temperature into a small EMF (millivolts).
- **Strain Gauge** — converts mechanical strain into a change in electrical resistance.
- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** — converts linear displacement into an AC voltage.
- **Piezoelectric Crystal** — converts force/pressure into an electrical charge.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark for each example = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): Functions & Elements of a Measurement System

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** With a neat block diagram, describe the **elements of a generalized measurement system**. Explain the function of each element.

**Model Solution:**

A generalized measurement system consists of **six functional elements** arranged in a cascade:

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MEASURAND] --> B[1. Primary Sensing]
    B --> C[2. Variable Conversion]
    C --> D[3. Variable Manipulation]
    D --> E[4. Data Transmission]
    E --> F[5. Data Presentation]
    F --> G[6. Data Storage Feedback]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
```

**Function of Each Element:**

1. **Primary Sensing Element:** First element that interacts with the measurand. It extracts a small portion of the measurand's energy to produce a related signal. *Example: Thermocouple hot junction, diaphragm in a pressure sensor.* **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Variable Conversion Element:** Converts the output of the primary sensing element into a more usable form, often changing the energy domain (e.g., mechanical to electrical). *Example: Strain gauge changes resistance due to mechanical strain.* **(1 mark)**

3. **Variable Manipulation Element:** Performs signal conditioning operations such as **amplification, attenuation, filtering, integration, differentiation, and linearization**. *Example: Op-amp instrumentation amplifier.* **(1.5 marks)**

4. **Data Transmission Element:** Carries the conditioned signal from the measurement site to the display/recording location. *Example: Shielded cable, optical fiber, wireless RF link.* **(1 mark)**

5. **Data Presentation Element:** Provides a human-readable display or hard-copy record. *Example: Digital voltmeter, LCD display, strip chart recorder.* **(1 mark)**

6. **Data Storage / Feedback Element:** Stores the data for later retrieval or feeds it back to a controller. *Example: Microcontroller RAM, PLC register, data logger.* **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Explain the **three primary functions** of a measurement system with suitable industrial examples. Discuss the difference between **indicating** and **recording** instruments with examples.

**Model Solution:**

The three primary functions are:

**1. Indicating Function:**  
The instrument gives a real-time display of the current value of the measurand.  
**Examples:** Ammeter in a switchboard, speedometer of a car, digital weighing scale at a shop.  
The operator reads the value *only* at the moment of inspection — no historical record is kept. **(2 marks)**

**2. Recording Function:**  
The instrument produces a permanent trace or log of how the measurand varies with time.  
**Examples:**  
- Seismograph records ground vibrations over hours/days.  
- Strip chart recorder in a power plant logs steam pressure 24×7.  
- Data logger in a cold-storage warehouse logs temperature continuously. **(2.5 marks)**

**3. Controlling Function:**  
The measured value is used as feedback to a controller that automatically regulates a process variable to a desired setpoint.  
**Examples:**  
- **Thermostat:** Measures room temperature, switches heater ON/OFF.  
- **Cruise Control:** Measures vehicle speed, adjusts throttle.  
- **PID Controller** in a chemical reactor maintaining pH at 7.0. **(2.5 marks)**

**Indicating vs. Recording Instruments:**

| Aspect | Indicating | Recording |
|--------|------------|-----------|
| **Purpose** | Show instantaneous value | Document time-history |
| **Output** | Pointer on dial / numeric display | Graph / digital log |
| **Memory** | None | Permanent storage |
| **Use** | Quick readouts | Trend analysis, compliance, audits |
| **Example** | Analog voltmeter, BP monitor | ECG machine, chart recorder |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Applications & Dynamic Response

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q:** Describe **five major applications** of measurement systems in modern engineering. For each, state the measurand and a typical sensor used.

**Model Solution:**

| S.No | Application Domain | Measurand | Typical Sensor |
|------|-------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Industrial Process Control** (Chemical plant) | Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Level | RTD, Strain Gauge, Orifice + dPT, Capacitive Probe |
| 2 | **Automotive Engine Management** (ECU) | Crankshaft position, MAP, Air-fuel ratio, O₂ | Hall sensor, Piezoresistive, Zirconia O₂ sensor |
| 3 | **Medical Patient Monitoring** (ICU) | ECG, SpO₂, Blood pressure, Respiration | Ag/AgCl electrodes, Pulse oximeter (LED+Photodiode), Cuff |
| 4 | **Aerospace Altimetry** | Atmospheric pressure | Capacitive aneroid + MEMS pressure sensor |
| 5 | **Environmental Pollution Monitoring** | PM2.5, SO₂, NOx, CO₂ | Laser scattering, Electrochemical cells, NDIR sensor |

**Detailed Explanation (1.4 marks each):**

1. **Industrial Process Control:** Modern plants use Distributed Control Systems (DCS) with hundreds of measurement loops. A temperature transmitter using an **RTD (PT100)** sends a 4–20 mA signal to a PID controller that modulates a steam valve. **(1.4 marks)**

2. **Automotive Engine Management:** An **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** receives inputs from oxygen sensors (lambda sensor), mass airflow (MAF), and manifold absolute pressure (MAP) sensors at kHz rates, computing fuel injection timing within microseconds. **(1.4 marks)**

3. **Medical Patient Monitoring:** Multi-parameter monitors in ICUs simultaneously track **ECG (electrodes), SpO₂ (fingertip pulse oximeter), NIBP (oscillometric cuff), and respiration (impedance pneumography)** with audible alarms. **(1.4 marks)**

4. **Aerospace Altimetry:** A **capacitive pressure sensor** measures static atmospheric pressure, which is converted to altitude using the ISA (International Standard Atmosphere) model. Modern aircraft also use **GPS-aided altimetry** for redundancy. **(1.4 marks)**

5. **Environmental Monitoring:** **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) sensors** measure CO₂ by absorption of IR at 4.26 µm wavelength. **PM2.5 sensors** use laser light scattering to count particles. **(1.4 marks)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q:** A first-order temperature measurement system has a time constant of 8 s and static sensitivity of 0.5 mV/°C. It is suddenly placed in a bath at 100°C (initial temperature was 0°C).  
**(i)** Find the output voltage at $t = 4 \text{ s}$, $t = 8 \text{ s}$, and $t = 32 \text{ s}$.  
**(ii)** What is the steady-state output voltage?

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\tau = 8 \text{ s}$
- $K = 0.5 \text{ mV/}^\circ\text{C}$
- $X_0 = 100^\circ\text{C}$ (step magnitude)
- Initial condition: $y(0) = 0$

**Formula:**

$$
y(t) = K X_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

**Final value:**

$$
y(\infty) = K X_0 = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \text{ mV}
$$

**(i) Calculations:**

**At $t = 4 \text{ s}$:** $t/\tau = 4/8 = 0.5$

$$
y(4) = 50 \left(1 - e^{-0.5}\right) = 50 \times (1 - 0.6065) = 50 \times 0.3935 = 19.67 \text{ mV}
$$

**At $t = 8 \text{ s}$:** $t/\tau = 8/8 = 1$

$$
y(8) = 50 \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = 50 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 50 \times 0.6321 = 31.61 \text{ mV}
$$

*(Confirms definition: at $t=\tau$, output reaches 63.2% of final value.)* **(2 marks)**

**At $t = 32 \text{ s}$:** $t/\tau = 32/8 = 4$

$$
y(32) = 50 \left(1 - e^{-4}\right) = 50 \times (1 - 0.0183) = 50 \times 0.9817 = 49.08 \text{ mV}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Steady-state output:**

$$
y_{ss} = 50 \text{ mV}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Tabulated Results:**

| Time (s) | $t/\tau$ | $e^{-t/\tau}$ | Output $y(t)$ (mV) |
|----------|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| 0 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 0.5 | 0.607 | 19.67 |
| 8 | 1.0 | 0.368 | 31.61 |
| 16 | 2.0 | 0.135 | 43.23 |
| 32 | 4.0 | 0.018 | 49.08 |
| ∞ | ∞ | 0.000 | **50.00 (steady state)** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 3 Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "Range" and "Span":** Range is the *region* (e.g., 0–100°C); Span is the *difference* (100°C). Examiners *specifically* deduct for this swap. **(–1 mark)**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the 63.2% definition of time constant:** A classic 3-mark question asks *"Define time constant and its significance."* Writing only the equation without stating "the time to reach 63.2% of final value" loses 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up linearity error with reading error:** Always express linearity as % of **Full Scale (FS)**, not the measured value. Many students write `% of reading` and lose 1–2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows or labels:** A block diagram must show **signal flow direction (arrows) and block labels**. Boxes alone fetch only partial credit.
> 
> 5. **In first-order derivations, omitting initial conditions:** A derivation without stating $y(0) = 0$ is considered incomplete in KTU valuation.
> 
> 6. **Confusing "Sensor" with "Transducer":** A *sensor* senses a physical quantity; a *transducer* converts one form of energy to another. Every sensor is a transducer, but not every transducer is a sensor. Examiners love testing this distinction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

### 🔑 Key Concepts to Memorize

- **Measurement** = Comparing an unknown quantity with a known standard → pure number.
- **Three primary functions:** Indicating, Recording, Controlling.
- **Six elements of a generalized measurement system:** Primary Sensing → Variable Conversion → Variable Manipulation → Data Transmission → Data Presentation → Storage/Feedback.
- **Three Doebelin stages:** Sensor Stage → Signal Conditioning Stage → Output Stage.

### 📊 Static Characteristics (Definition + Formula)

- **Sensitivity:** $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ (slope of input-output curve)
- **Range:** Region between minimum and maximum measurable values
- **Span:** Algebraic difference $x_{max} - x_{min}$
- **Linearity:** Maximum deviation from best-fit line, in % of FS
- **Hysteresis:** Output difference for same input from opposite directions
- **Dead Zone:** Largest input change that produces no output change
- **Resolution:** Smallest detectable input increment
- **Drift:** Output change with time for constant input (thermal, aging)

### ⚡ Dynamic Characteristics (First-Order Essentials)

- **Time Constant $\tau$:** Time to reach 63.2% of final value
- **Steady-state reached** at $t \approx 4\tau$ (98.2%)
- **First-order step response:** $y(t) = K X_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$
- **Second-order natural frequency $\omega_n$:** Frequency of undamped oscillation
- **Damping ratio $\zeta$:** Determines overshoot and settling behavior
  - $\zeta = 0$: Undamped oscillation
  - $\zeta = 1$: Critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory)
  - $\zeta > 1$: Overdamped
  - $0 < \zeta < 1$: Underdamped (oscillatory)

### 🏭 Application Domains (Industrial Memory Map)

| Domain | Key Measurand | Common Sensor |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Process Control | T, P, F, L | RTD, Strain gauge, Orifice |
| Automotive | Speed, MAP, O₂ | Hall, Piezoresistive, Zirconia |
| Medical | ECG, BP, SpO₂ | Electrodes, Cuff, Photodiode |
| Aerospace | Altitude, Attitude | Capacitive, MEMS gyro |
| Environmental | PM, CO₂, NOx | Laser, NDIR, Electrochemical |
| Consumer | Touch, Light, Motion | Capacitive, Photodiode, MEMS |

### ⚙️ Cascaded System Property

$$
K_{total} = K_1 \times K_2 \times \ldots \times K_n \quad \text{(Multiplicative sensitivity rule)}
$$

### 🔁 Closed-Loop Advantage

$$
T(s) = \frac{K_{forward}}{1 + K_{forward} \cdot \beta} \xrightarrow{K_{forward}\beta \gg 1} \frac{1}{\beta}
$$

**Closed-loop systems depend only on the feedback element — this is the foundation of all precision instruments.**

### 🎯 Last-Minute KTU Power Lines

- *"You cannot control what you cannot measure."* — Law of Instrumentation.
- *Every sensor is a transducer; not every transducer is a sensor.*
- *Time constant = 63.2% rule.*
- *Linearity is always expressed as % of full scale (FS).*
- *Cascade sensitivity is the **product** of individual sensitivities.*
- *Closed-loop systems achieve accuracy via the **feedback element**, not the forward path.*

> **Best of luck with your KTU Module 3 preparation! 🚀**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Instruments and Measurement Systems: Measurement System Performance, Static Calibration, Static Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT611 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Instruments and Measurement Systems: Measurement System Performance, Static Calibration, Static Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Measurement System & Its Performance — Formal Definition

A **Measurement System** is a collection of physical components and devices assembled to acquire a physical signal (the *measurand*), condition it, and present it in a form (numerical, graphical, or visual) meaningful to a human observer or a controller.

**Measurement System Performance** refers to the *quantitative and qualitative behavior* of a measurement system when subjected to a known input. Performance is broadly classified into:

1. **Static Performance** — Behavior when the input is constant or varies so slowly that time-dependent (dynamic) effects are negligible.
2. **Dynamic Performance** — Behavior when the input changes rapidly with time (covered in later modules).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 3):** This module emphasizes **static performance** indicators, the procedure of **static calibration**, and the **static characteristics** (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, resolution, drift, etc.) of measuring instruments.

## 1.2 Static Calibration — Definition

**Static Calibration** is the experimental procedure by which the *output* of an instrument is compared against a *reference standard* of known accuracy, over the entire intended measurement range, under controlled static (steady-state) environmental conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Calibration ≠ Adjustment.** Calibration establishes the *relationship* between input and output (calibration curve), while adjustment modifies the instrument to bring readings within tolerance.

The result of static calibration is a **Calibration Curve**: a plot of the *true reference input* (x-axis) versus the *indicated output* (y-axis) of the instrument.

## 1.3 Static Characteristics — Intuitive Overview

**Static Characteristics** are the attributes that describe the behavior of an instrument when the measurand is either **constant** or **changing very slowly** (i.e., when all time-dependent transient effects have decayed to zero).

### Real-World Analogy: The Bathroom Weighing Scale

Imagine you step onto a digital bathroom weighing scale every morning:

- **Accuracy** — Does the scale read *exactly* 70.0 kg when a calibrated 70.0 kg standard mass is placed on it? *(Closeness to the true value)*
- **Precision** — If you step on and off 5 times, do you get the *same* reading? *(Repeatability / closeness among readings)*
- **Sensitivity** — Does the last digit flicker wildly when you breathe? *(Output change per unit input change)*
- **Linearity** — Does a 10 kg mass and a 20 kg mass each give exactly 1/2 and full-scale reading, or do they deviate? *(Proportionality of output to input)*
- **Hysteresis** — If you slowly place a 50 kg mass and then slowly remove it, do the readings during loading and unloading *coincide*? *(Path-dependence)*
- **Drift** — Does the scale show 70.1 kg today, 70.3 kg next week, with no one standing on it? *(Slow change with time)*
- **Resolution** — Can the scale distinguish between 70.05 kg and 70.06 kg, or only between 70.0 and 70.1? *(Smallest detectable change)*
- **Dead Zone / Threshold** — Does the scale show "0.0 kg" until you place at least 0.2 kg? *(Range of input producing no output change)*

A **good** measurement system scores high on *all* of the above simultaneously. A **mediocre** one may be precise (repeatable) but inaccurate, or accurate on average but imprecise.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** In exam questions, the phrase "static characteristics" almost always expects definitions of **accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, and resolution**, along with their mathematical expressions. Memorize the difference between *accuracy* and *precision* — it is the single most-asked distinction.

## 1.4 Generalized Block Diagram of a Measurement System

A typical measurement chain consists of three primary functional stages:

$$
\text{Primary Sensing Element} \rightarrow \text{Variable Conversion Element} \rightarrow \text{Data Presentation Element}
$$

1. **Primary Sensing Element (Sensor / Transducer):** Directly interacts with the measurand and produces an initial signal (e.g., strain gauge bonded to a cantilever beam).
2. **Variable Conversion Element (Signal Conditioner):** Converts the initial signal into a more usable form — amplifies, filters, linearizes, or digitizes it (e.g., Wheatstone bridge + amplifier).
3. **Data Presentation Element (Display / Output):** Renders the conditioned signal for human or machine interpretation (e.g., digital display, chart recorder, PLC input).

A static characteristic can be attributed either to the **entire system** or to **individual stages**, depending on which element dominates the non-ideality.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Definitions of Key Static Characteristics

### 2.1.1 Accuracy
The closeness of an instrument's *indicated value* to the *true value* of the measurand.

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{True Value}}{\text{Indicated Value}}
\quad \text{or equivalently} \quad
\text{Error} = \text{Indicated Value} - \text{True Value}
$$

- Expressed as a **percentage of full-scale (% FS)** or **percentage of reading (% RD)**.
- **Inaccuracy** = (Error / True Value) × 100%.
- **High accuracy = small error.**

### 2.1.2 Precision
The degree of *mutual agreement* (repeatability) among a series of independent measurements of the same quantity under prescribed conditions. **Precision is a property of the instrument, NOT of the measurement.**

- High precision does **not** imply high accuracy.
- A precise instrument may have a *systematic bias* (offset error) and still be precise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accuracy vs Precision (KTU favourite):**
> - *Accuracy* = closeness to **true** value (correctness).
> - *Precision* = closeness among **repeated** readings (consistency).

### 2.1.3 Sensitivity (K)
Defined as the *ratio of change in output* to the *corresponding change in input* that caused it.

$$
K = \frac{\Delta \theta_{\text{out}}}{\Delta \theta_{\text{in}}}
$$

Units: (output unit) / (input unit). For a linear instrument, $K$ is a constant equal to the slope of the calibration curve.

### 2.1.4 Resolution (Discrimination)
The *smallest change* in the measurand that produces a detectable change in the instrument's output. Below the resolution threshold, the output is indistinguishable.

For a digital display of $n$ decimal places over full scale $F$:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{F}{10^{n} - 1}
$$

### 2.1.5 Threshold
The *minimum input* magnitude required to produce a measurable output change. Below threshold, the output remains at zero (or its quiescent value).

### 2.1.6 Range and Span

- **Range:** The minimum to maximum input values over which the instrument is designed to operate (e.g., 0 °C to 200 °C).
- **Span (Full-Scale, FS):** The *algebraic difference* between the maximum and minimum input values.

$$
\text{Span} = X_{\max} - X_{\min}
$$

### 2.1.7 Linearity
An instrument is *linear* if its calibration curve can be well-approximated by a **straight line**.

**Two common reference lines:**

- **Independent Linearity (Best-Fit Zero-Adjusted Line):** A straight line placed such that the maximum positive and negative deviations are equal in magnitude.
- **Terminal (End-Point) Linearity:** A straight line connecting the calibration points at the lower-range and upper-range end-points.

**Linearity Error (% FS):**

$$
\% \text{ Linearity} = \frac{(y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{straight line}})_{\max}}{Y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100
$$

### 2.1.8 Hysteresis
The *maximum difference* in output readings obtained when the same input is approached **first from one direction** (increasing) and **then from the other** (decreasing).

$$
\text{Hysteresis Error} = \theta_{\text{up}} - \theta_{\text{down}}
\quad \text{at the same input } x
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Hysteresis in instruments is often caused by *mechanical friction*, *magnetic hysteresis* in ferromagnets, *elastic after-effect* in springs, or *thermal lag*.

### 2.1.9 Dead Band (Dead Zone)
The *range of input values* over which the output remains unchanged. It is essentially the *threshold region*. A dead band may exist at the low end (threshold) or anywhere in the range.

### 2.1.10 Repeatability
The ability of an instrument to reproduce the *same output* when the *same input* is applied repeatedly under the *same conditions*, by the *same observer*, with the *same instrument*, in the *same location*, over a *short period*.

$$
\% \text{ Repeatability} = \frac{\Delta R_{\max}}{Y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100
$$

where $\Delta R_{\max}$ is the maximum spread among repeated readings.

### 2.1.11 Reproducibility
The closeness of agreement among measurements of the same quantity when conditions are **changed** (different observer, instrument, location, time, etc.). Reproducibility is *weaker* than repeatability.

### 2.1.12 Drift
An *unwanted slow change* in output with time, even when the input is held constant.

**Types of drift:**
- **Zero Drift:** Output shifts when input is zero.
- **Sensitivity Drift:** The slope $K$ changes with time.
- **Thermal Drift:** Drift caused by ambient temperature changes.
- **Zener Drift / Aging Drift:** Long-term material-property changes.

Drift is often quantified per unit time (e.g., $\mu$V/hour) or per °C (e.g., ppm/°C).

### 2.1.13 Stability
The ability of an instrument to *retain its performance characteristics* over a long period. An instrument with *low drift* has *high stability*.

### 2.1.14 Static Sensitivity & Static Error

The total static error $e_s$ of an instrument reading $\theta_m$ versus true value $\theta_t$:

$$
e_s = \theta_m - \theta_t
$$

For a *non-linear* instrument approximated by a polynomial:

$$
\theta_{\text{out}} = a_0 + a_1 \theta_{\text{in}} + a_2 \theta_{\text{in}}^{2} + a_3 \theta_{\text{in}}^{3} + \dots
$$

- $a_0$ = zero offset (ideally zero).
- $a_1$ = ideal sensitivity (ideally constant).
- $a_2, a_3, \dots$ = non-linearity terms (ideally zero).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Static Characteristic | Defining Equation | Typical Unit / Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity $K$ | $K = \Delta \theta_{\text{out}} \,/\, \Delta \theta_{\text{in}}$ | (output unit)/(input unit) | Sensor design, gain selection |
| 2 | Static Error $e_s$ | $e_s = \theta_m - \theta_t$ | Same as output unit | Calibration, error budgeting |
| 3 | % Error | $\%e = (e_s / \theta_t) \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Specification sheets |
| 4 | Span (FS) | $\text{Span} = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | Input unit | Instrument selection |
| 5 | Resolution (digital) | $\text{Res} = F / (10^n - 1)$ | Input unit | ADC selection |
| 6 | % Linearity | $(\Delta y_{\max}/Y_{FS}) \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Quality control |
| 7 | Hysteresis Error | $e_h = \theta_{\text{up}} - \theta_{\text{down}}$ | Output unit | Material hysteresis, friction |
| 8 | % Repeatability | $(\Delta R_{\max}/Y_{FS}) \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Manufacturing test |
| 9 | Drift Rate | $\Delta \theta / \Delta t$ | (output unit)/time | Long-term monitoring |
| 10 | Calibration Curve Fit | $\theta_{\text{out}} = a_0 + a_1 \theta_{\text{in}} + \epsilon$ | Polynomial | Sensor linearization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table formatting:** All absolute-value and ratio bars in the equations above use the LaTeX forms `\vert` or `\mid` so that the markdown table parser does not break on a raw `|` character.

## 2.3 Why These Characteristics Matter in Engineering

- **Process Control:** A poorly calibrated temperature sensor in a chemical reactor can cause an entire batch to fail. *Accuracy and drift* dominate.
- **Aerospace Instrumentation:** Flight-control sensors must have *low hysteresis* and *high repeatability*; failure is catastrophic.
- **Medical Devices:** Patient monitoring requires *high resolution* and *low dead zone*.
- **Manufacturing QA:** Coordinate-Measuring Machines (CMMs) demand *high precision and reproducibility* across days of operation.

> [!TIP]
> Always specify **conditions** when quoting a static characteristic. e.g., "Repeatability of ±0.05 % FS at 25 °C, 24-hour period." KTU exam answers that omit conditions may lose marks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Sensitivity for a Multi-Element Cascade

### Problem Setup
A measurement system has three cascaded stages: a sensor (gain $K_1$), a signal conditioner (gain $K_2$), and a display unit (gain $K_3$). Derive the *overall sensitivity* of the system.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the measurand be $x$ (input to stage 1), and let the output of stage $i$ be $y_i$.

**Step 1 — Output of sensor (Stage 1):**
By definition of sensitivity, the output of the first stage is proportional to the input, with proportionality constant $K_1$:

$$
y_1 = K_1 \, x
$$

**Step 2 — Output of conditioner (Stage 2):**
The conditioner input is $y_1$ and its sensitivity is $K_2$:

$$
y_2 = K_2 \, y_1
$$

**Step 3 — Output of display (Stage 3):**
The display input is $y_2$ and its sensitivity is $K_3$:

$$
y_3 = K_3 \, y_2
$$

**Step 4 — Combine in cascade:**

$$
y_3 = K_3 \, y_2 = K_3 \, (K_2 \, y_1) = K_3 \, K_2 \, (K_1 \, x)
$$

$$
\therefore \; y_3 = (K_1 \, K_2 \, K_3) \, x
$$

**Step 5 — Overall Sensitivity $K_{\text{total}}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,K_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{y_3}{x} = K_1 \, K_2 \, K_3 \,}
$$

**Conclusion:** The sensitivities of cascaded linear stages **multiply**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *key result* for KTU problems involving multi-element systems. For $n$ cascaded stages: $K_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} K_i$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Linearity Error from a Calibration Curve

### Problem Setup
A pressure transducer is calibrated using five standard pressures. The ideal (reference) line passes through the origin with slope $K_{\text{ideal}}$. The actual readings are tabulated below. Compute the linearity error as a % of full-scale output.

| Standard Pressure $P$ (bar) | Ideal Output $y_{\text{ideal}}$ (mV) | Actual Output $y_{\text{actual}}$ (mV) |
|---|---|---|
| 0   | 0    | 0    |
| 2   | 100  | 102  |
| 4   | 200  | 199  |
| 6   | 300  | 302  |
| 8   | 400  | 397  |
| 10  | 500  | 501  |

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Establish the ideal line:**
The reference (best-fit straight line through origin) is $y_{\text{ideal}} = 50 P$ (mV/bar), so $K_{\text{ideal}} = 50$ mV/bar, and $Y_{\text{FS}} = 500$ mV at $P = 10$ bar.

**Step 2 — Compute the deviation at each calibration point:**

$$
\Delta y_i = y_{\text{actual}, i} - y_{\text{ideal}, i}
$$

For $P = 2$ bar: $\Delta y = 102 - 100 = +2$ mV
For $P = 4$ bar: $\Delta y = 199 - 200 = -1$ mV
For $P = 6$ bar: $\Delta y = 302 - 300 = +2$ mV
For $P = 8$ bar: $\Delta y = 397 - 400 = -3$ mV
For $P = 10$ bar: $\Delta y = 501 - 500 = +1$ mV

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum absolute deviation:**

$$
\Delta y_{\max} = \max(\, \vert 2 \vert, \vert -1 \vert, \vert 2 \vert, \vert -3 \vert, \vert 1 \vert \,) = 3 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the linearity error as % of full-scale:**

$$
\% \text{ Linearity} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{Y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Linearity} = \frac{3}{500} \times 100 = 0.6 \,\%
$$

**Step 5 — State the result:**

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Linearity Error} = 0.6 \,\% \text{ of Full Scale (FS)} \,}
$$

**Conclusion:** The transducer has a maximum non-linearity of ±0.6 % FS, which is acceptable for most industrial-grade pressure measurements (typical spec: ±1 % FS).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Hysteresis Error from Up-Down Calibration

### Problem Setup
A thermistor temperature sensor is calibrated by *increasing* temperature from 0 °C to 100 °C in 20 °C steps (up-scale), and then *decreasing* it back (down-scale). The readings are:

| True Temperature $T$ (°C) | Up-Scale Reading $R_{\uparrow}$ (Ω) | Down-Scale Reading $R_{\downarrow}$ (Ω) |
|---|---|---|
| 0   | 100.0 | 100.0 |
| 20  | 90.0  | 91.5  |
| 40  | 80.2  | 81.0  |
| 60  | 70.0  | 71.5  |
| 80  | 60.3  | 61.2  |
| 100 | 50.0  | 50.0  |

Find the maximum hysteresis error.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute hysteresis error at each calibration point:**

$$
e_h(T) = R_{\uparrow}(T) - R_{\downarrow}(T)
$$

| $T$ (°C) | $e_h$ (Ω) |
|---|---|
| 20 | 90.0 − 91.5 = −1.5 |
| 40 | 80.2 − 81.0 = −0.8 |
| 60 | 70.0 − 71.5 = −1.5 |
| 80 | 60.3 − 61.2 = −0.9 |

**Step 2 — Take the maximum absolute value:**

$$
e_{h,\max} = \max(\, \vert -1.5 \vert, \vert -0.8 \vert, \vert -1.5 \vert, \vert -0.9 \vert \,) = 1.5 \,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Express as % of full-scale output:**

$$
\% \text{ Hysteresis} = \frac{e_{h,\max}}{Y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100 = \frac{1.5}{100.0} \times 100 = 1.5 \,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Maximum Hysteresis Error} = 1.5 \,\Omega \;\;(1.5 \,\% \text{ FS})\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In a real calibration lab, the up-scale and down-scale curves would be plotted on the same graph; the *maximum vertical separation* between them at any input value is the hysteresis error.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Overall Static Error Under Multi-Source Errors

### Problem Setup
A measurement system has three sources of static error: zero offset $e_0$, scale-factor (gain) error $e_K$, and non-linearity error $e_{NL}$. The instrument output model is:

$$
y_{\text{out}} = a_0 + a_1 \, x + a_2 \, x^2
$$

where $x$ is the input. Find the expression for *total static error* relative to an ideal linear system $y_{\text{ideal}} = K \, x$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the ideal response:**

$$
y_{\text{ideal}}(x) = K \, x
$$

where $K$ is the *nominal* design sensitivity (the constant of proportionality the system was designed to have).

**Step 2 — Define the actual response:**

$$
y_{\text{actual}}(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2
$$

**Step 3 — Define the static error at any input $x$:**

$$
e_s(x) = y_{\text{actual}}(x) - y_{\text{ideal}}(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 - K x
$$

**Step 4 — Group terms to identify each error source:**

$$
e_s(x) = \underbrace{a_0}_{\text{Zero Error}} + \underbrace{(a_1 - K)\,x}_{\text{Gain Error}} + \underbrace{a_2 x^2}_{\text{Non-linearity Error}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$
\boxed{\,e_s(x) = e_0 + e_K \cdot x + e_{NL} \cdot x^2\,}
$$

where
- $e_0 = a_0$ (zero offset),
- $e_K = a_1 - K$ (gain error),
- $e_{NL} = a_2$ (non-linearity coefficient).

**Conclusion:** The total static error is a **sum of three physically meaningful contributions**: an input-independent *zero error*, an input-linear *gain error*, and an input-quadratic *non-linearity error*. This decomposition is the foundation of *error budgeting* in metrology.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Combined Sensitivity and Resolution

### Problem Setup
A linear displacement transducer with a span of 0 to 100 mm produces an output voltage of 0 to 5 V. A 12-bit ADC is connected to its output.

**(a)** Find the sensitivity of the transducer.
**(b)** Find the resolution of the measurement system (in mm).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Sensitivity:**

By definition:

$$
K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}
$$

$$
K = \frac{5 - 0}{100 - 0} = \frac{5}{100} = 0.05 \text{ V/mm}
$$

**Part (b) — Resolution of the ADC:**
For a 12-bit ADC over 5 V range, the smallest distinguishable voltage step is:

$$
\Delta V_{\min} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{5}{4095} \approx 1.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}
$$

Converting this voltage back to equivalent input displacement:

$$
\Delta x_{\min} = \frac{\Delta V_{\min}}{K} = \frac{1.221 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05} \approx 0.0244 \text{ mm} \approx 24.4 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,K = 0.05 \text{ V/mm}, \quad \text{System Resolution} \approx 24.4 \,\mu\text{m}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical KTU Part-A / Part-B sub-question pattern. The *transducer* defines sensitivity; the *ADC* defines resolution of the overall system.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Static Characteristics Evaluation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the **functional flow** by which static characteristics are derived from raw experimental calibration data. This is the *block-level architecture* of static instrument analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measurand Source e.g. Reference Pressure Standard] --> B[Instrument Under Test IUT]
    B --> C[Raw Output Reading]
    A --> D[Reference True Value]
    C --> E[Static Calibration Block Compare to Reference]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Calibration Data Table]
    F --> G[Best Fit Straight Line]
    G --> H[Static Characteristics Extractor]
    F --> H
    H --> H1[Sensitivity K]
    H --> H2[Linearity Error NL]
    H --> H3[Hysteresis Error H]
    H --> H4[Repeatability R]
    H --> H5[Resolution Res]
    H --> H6[Drift Rate D]
    H1 --> I[Performance Report]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow begins with a *known* measurand fed simultaneously to the IUT and to a *reference* standard. The comparison yields a calibration table, which feeds a *static-characteristics extractor*, producing individual metrics that combine into a final performance report.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Static Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Primary Sensor Transducer] --> S[Signal Conditioner Amplify Filter]
    S --> T[Transmitter / Normalization Stage]
    T --> D[Data Presentation Display / Recorder]
    P --> P1[Static Characteristic: Sensitivity K1]
    S --> S1[Static Characteristic: Linearity Drift]
    T --> T1[Static Characteristic: Gain Stability]
    D --> D1[Static Characteristic: Resolution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each block of a measurement chain contributes its *own* static characteristics, and the overall system characteristics are a *cascade* combination (per Section 3.1).

## 4.3 Calibration Curve — Schematic Concept

The following conceptual schematic (rendered as a Mermaid block) represents the *plot of output vs input* and how static characteristics are read off it.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Ideal vs Actual Calibration]
        direction LR
        A1[Input x increases from Xmin to Xmax]
        A1 --> A2[Output yideal K times x straight line through origin]
        A1 --> A3[Output yactual curved with deviations NL and hysteresis loop]
    end
    A2 --> B[Linearity Error is max vertical gap between A2 and A3]
    A3 --> C[Hysteresis Error is max vertical gap between up and down curves]
    A2 --> D[Sensitivity K is the slope of the straight line A2]
    A1 --> E[Resolution is the smallest distinguishable increment on x]
```

> [!TIP]
> If the Mermaid renderer is unavailable, the same schematic is conveyed in a sentence: *"The calibration curve is a graph of indicated output (y) versus true input (x); sensitivity is its slope, linearity is its straightness, hysteresis is the gap between up- and down-traces, and resolution is the smallest input step the curve can resolve."*

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define the following static characteristics of a measurement system: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Sensitivity. Distinguish clearly between accuracy and precision. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Accuracy:** The closeness of an *indicated* value to the *true* value of the measurand. Quantitatively, accuracy is often expressed as

$$
\text{Inaccuracy} = \frac{\text{Indicated Value} - \text{True Value}}{\text{True Value}} \times 100 \,\%
$$

A high-accuracy instrument has a small inaccuracy.

**(ii) Precision:** The degree of *mutual agreement* among a series of independent measurements of the *same* quantity, obtained under prescribed conditions. A precise instrument gives *consistent* (repeatable) readings, but they may all be offset from the true value.

**(iii) Sensitivity ($K$):** The ratio of the change in output ($\Delta y$) to the change in input ($\Delta x$) that produced it:

$$
K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}
$$

**Distinction (Accuracy vs Precision):**

| Property | Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| Refers to | True value | Repeated readings |
| Affected by | Systematic errors | Random errors |
| Improvement | Calibration, zero-set | Better resolution, controlled environment |

An instrument can be *precise without being accurate* (e.g., a miscalibrated digital caliper giving 50.05, 50.05, 50.05 mm for a true 50.00 mm) and *accurate on average without being precise* (e.g., readings 49.8, 50.2, 50.0 mm averaging to the true value). **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. Define the terms: (i) Hysteresis, (ii) Dead Zone, (iii) Drift, as applied to measuring instruments. Mention one cause of each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Hysteresis:** The phenomenon in which the *output* of an instrument for a given input depends on whether the input was approached from a *lower* or a *higher* value. Quantitatively, it is the maximum difference in output readings between the *up-scale* and *down-scale* traversal of the input.

$$
e_h = (y_{\text{up}} - y_{\text{down}})_{\max}
$$

**Cause:** Mechanical friction in moving parts, magnetic hysteresis in ferromagnetic cores, or elastic after-effect in springs.

**(ii) Dead Zone (Dead Band):** The range of input values *over which the output does not change* (or changes by an undetectable amount). It is effectively the *threshold* region extended over a finite input range.

**Cause:** Static friction (stiction) in pointer mechanisms, or backlash in gear trains.

**(iii) Drift:** A slow, unwanted change in the instrument output with *time*, even when the input remains constant. Drift may be *zero drift* (output shifts at zero input) or *sensitivity drift* (slope changes).

**Cause:** Aging of components, ambient temperature variations, or relaxation of mechanical stresses. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1). (a) Explain in detail the procedure of **static calibration** of a measuring instrument. With a neat sketch, describe a typical static calibration curve and explain how the various static characteristics are determined from it. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### (b) The following calibration data is obtained for a temperature transducer:

| True Temperature (°C) | Indicated Output (mV) |
|---|---|
| 0   | 0.0   |
| 20  | 10.2  |
| 40  | 19.8  |
| 60  | 30.5  |
| 80  | 39.7  |
| 100 | 50.1  |

**Determine: (i) the ideal sensitivity assuming a best-fit straight line through origin, (ii) the maximum linearity error as a % of full scale, and (iii) the static error at 60 °C.** (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Static Calibration Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition:** Static calibration is the experimental comparison of an instrument's output against a *reference standard* of known, higher accuracy, under controlled static (steady-state) environmental conditions, over the full input range.

**Step 2 — Procedure (in order):**

1. **Selection of Reference Standard:** Choose a calibration standard whose accuracy is at least *4 to 10 times better* than the instrument under test (IUT).
2. **Environmental Control:** Maintain temperature, humidity, and pressure at specified reference conditions (commonly 25 °C, 50 % RH).
3. **Connection & Warm-up:** Power on the IUT and the standard; allow sufficient warm-up time (typically 30 min) to eliminate thermal transients.
4. **Up-Scale Calibration:** Apply known inputs in *increasing* order from $X_{\min}$ to $X_{\max}$ in equal increments. Record the IUT output at each step. Repeat 3–5 times to assess repeatability.
5. **Down-Scale Calibration:** Apply inputs in *decreasing* order from $X_{\max}$ to $X_{\min}$. Record again.
6. **Tabulation:** Construct a calibration data table with columns for *true input, indicated output (up), indicated output (down)*.
7. **Plotting the Calibration Curve:** Plot indicated output (y) vs true input (x).
8. **Best-Fit Line:** Draw a best-fit straight line (least-squares regression or visual end-point method) through the data.
9. **Extraction of Characteristics:** Sensitivity (slope), linearity error (max deviation), hysteresis (max up-down gap), repeatability (max spread at each input), zero error (y-intercept), and resolution.

**Step 3 — Sketch of Calibration Curve:**

```
  Output (mV)
    |
    |                          *  (Xmax, Ymax)
    |                       *
    |                    *
    |                 *        <-- best-fit straight line (slope = K)
    |              *  /
    |           *   /
    |        *    /  <-- max deviation = linearity error
    |     *    /
    |  *    /
    |* /
    +------------------------------------> Input
   (Xmin, Ymin)
```

**Step 4 — Determination of characteristics (from the curve):**
- **Sensitivity $K$** = slope of the best-fit line.
- **Linearity error** = maximum vertical distance between the best-fit line and any calibration point, expressed as % of FS.
- **Hysteresis** = maximum vertical distance between the up-scale and down-scale curves at the same input.
- **Zero error** = y-axis intercept (output when input is nominally zero).
- **Span** = $X_{\max} - X_{\min}$.

**(Part (a) carries 7 marks — distribute as: definition 1, procedure 3, sketch 1, characteristics from curve 2.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Ideal sensitivity (best-fit line through origin):**
Using the *least-squares* formula for a line through the origin, $y = K x$:

$$
K = \frac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sum x_i^{2}}
$$

Compute the sum:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i y_i$ | $x_i^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0   | 0.0  | 0     | 0    |
| 20  | 10.2 | 204   | 400  |
| 40  | 19.8 | 792   | 1600 |
| 60  | 30.5 | 1830  | 3600 |
| 80  | 39.7 | 3176  | 6400 |
| 100 | 50.1 | 5010  | 10000 |
| **Sum** |     | **11012** | **22000** |

$$
K = \frac{11012}{22000} = 0.5005 \text{ mV/°C} \approx 0.5 \text{ mV/°C}
$$

**(ii) Maximum linearity error as % FS:**

Full-scale output $Y_{\text{FS}} = 50.1$ mV (at $x = 100$ °C).

Compute ideal output $y_{\text{ideal}} = K \cdot x = 0.5005 \, x$:

| $x$ | $y_{\text{actual}}$ | $y_{\text{ideal}}$ | $\Delta y = y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ideal}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0   | 0.0  | 0.0    | 0.0    |
| 20  | 10.2 | 10.01  | +0.19  |
| 40  | 19.8 | 20.02  | −0.22  |
| 60  | 30.5 | 30.03  | +0.47  |
| 80  | 39.7 | 40.04  | −0.34  |
| 100 | 50.1 | 50.05  | +0.05  |

Maximum absolute deviation:

$$
\Delta y_{\max} = 0.47 \text{ mV (at 60 °C)}
$$

Linearity error:

$$
\% \text{ Linearity} = \frac{0.47}{50.1} \times 100 \approx 0.94 \,\% \text{ FS}
$$

**(iii) Static error at 60 °C:**

$$
e_s(60) = y_{\text{actual}}(60) - y_{\text{ideal}}(60) = 30.5 - 30.03 = +0.47 \text{ mV}
$$

This is the *positive* deviation of the actual reading from the ideal value at 60 °C.

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{K \approx 0.5 \text{ mV/°C}, \quad \text{Linearity Error} \approx 0.94 \,\% \text{ FS}, \quad e_s(60) = +0.47 \text{ mV}}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2). (a) Define and explain with examples: **sensitivity, range, span, resolution, and threshold** as static characteristics of a measurement system. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3]`

#### (b) A pressure gauge has a range of 0 to 10 bar and an output of 0 to 100 mV. A 10-bit ADC is used to digitize this output. Calculate: (i) the sensitivity of the gauge, (ii) the resolution of the ADC in mV, and (iii) the smallest pressure change that can be detected by the overall system. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Definitions with Examples (7 marks):**

1. **Sensitivity ($K$):** Ratio of output change to input change.
   - *Example:* A thermocouple producing 40 µV/°C — for every 1 °C rise in temperature, the output voltage rises by 40 µV.

2. **Range:** The *minimum* to *maximum* input values the instrument is designed to measure.
   - *Example:* A clinical thermometer with range 35 °C to 42 °C.

3. **Span (Full Scale, FS):** The *algebraic difference* between the maximum and minimum input values.
   - *Example:* For the same thermometer, span = 42 − 35 = 7 °C.

4. **Resolution:** The *smallest* change in the measurand that produces a detectable change in output.
   - *Example:* A digital voltmeter displaying 0.001 V has a resolution of 1 mV; a meter displaying 0.01 V has a resolution of 10 mV.

5. **Threshold:** The *minimum* input magnitude required to initiate a measurable output change.
   - *Example:* A force gauge may show 0.0 N until a force of at least 0.1 N is applied — the threshold is 0.1 N.

**(Distribute 7 marks: ~1.4 marks per definition with one example each.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Pressure range: 0 to 10 bar
- Output range: 0 to 100 mV
- ADC: 10-bit

**(i) Sensitivity of the gauge:**

$$
K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{100 - 0}{10 - 0} = 10 \text{ mV/bar}
$$

**(ii) Resolution of the ADC in mV:**

For a 10-bit ADC ($2^{10} = 1024$ levels) over a 0 to 100 mV range:

$$
\Delta V_{\min} = \frac{100 \text{ mV}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{100}{1023} \approx 0.0978 \text{ mV}
$$

**(iii) Smallest detectable pressure change:**

Convert ADC voltage resolution to equivalent pressure using sensitivity:

$$
\Delta P_{\min} = \frac{\Delta V_{\min}}{K} = \frac{0.0978 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/bar}} \approx 0.00978 \text{ bar} \approx 9.78 \times 10^{-3} \text{ bar}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{K = 10 \text{ mV/bar}, \quad \Delta V_{\min} \approx 0.098 \text{ mV}, \quad \Delta P_{\min} \approx 9.78 \times 10^{-3} \text{ bar}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 3 Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating accuracy with precision** — KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. A vague "accuracy means correct measurement" answer is worth 0.5 of 3 marks; a precise contrast with *systematic* vs *random* error origin fetches full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the FS reference in %-error expressions** — Linearity, hysteresis, and repeatability errors must be expressed as a *percentage of full-scale (FS)*, not as a percentage of the local reading. Writing "0.6 %" without specifying "of FS" loses 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Sign convention for static error** — Always state whether the error is *positive* (indicated > true) or *negative* (indicated < true). Examiners reward explicit sign.
> 4. **Calibration vs Adjustment confusion** — Calibration is a comparison; adjustment is a modification. Mixing up the two costs 1 mark in part-(a) definition questions.
> 5. **Unit omission in sensitivity** — Always write the unit (e.g., mV/°C, mV/bar, V/mm). A bare number for sensitivity is marked down.
> 6. **Forgetting to specify environmental conditions** — Static characteristics are *only* valid at stated conditions (temperature, pressure, humidity). Omitting this in a "compare two instruments" answer loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 Static Characteristics**

- ✅ **Static characteristics** describe instrument behavior when input is *constant* or *very slowly varying* (steady-state, no time effects).
- ✅ **Static calibration** = comparison against a *reference standard*; produces a *calibration curve*.
- ✅ **Accuracy** = closeness to *true* value; quantified by *inaccuracy* (%).
- ✅ **Precision** = mutual agreement among *repeated* readings; a property of the *instrument*, not the *measurement*.
- ✅ **Sensitivity $K$** = $\Delta y / \Delta x$; constant for a linear instrument; **multiplies** in cascade.
- ✅ **Span** = $X_{\max} - X_{\min}$; full-scale (FS) output corresponds to span.
- ✅ **Resolution** = smallest detectable input change; for an $n$-bit digital system: $\text{Res} = \text{FS} / (2^n - 1)$.
- ✅ **Threshold** = minimum input for detectable output.
- ✅ **Linearity error** = max deviation from best-fit line, expressed as **% of FS**.
- ✅ **Hysteresis** = max gap between up-scale and down-scale curves; caused by friction, magnetic hysteresis, elastic after-effect.
- ✅ **Dead zone** = range of input over which output *does not* change.
- ✅ **Drift** = slow unwanted change in output with time at constant input; types: zero drift, sensitivity drift, thermal drift.
- ✅ **Repeatability** = same conditions, same observer, short time span; quantified by max spread.
- ✅ **Reproducibility** = changed conditions (observer, instrument, location); weaker than repeatability.
- ✅ **Stability** = long-term retention of characteristics; inversely related to drift.
- ✅ **Polynomial model:** $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \dots$; $a_0$ = zero error, $a_1$ = sensitivity, $a_2, a_3$ = non-linearity terms.
- ✅ **Cascade rule:** $K_{\text{total}} = K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdots K_n$.
- ✅ Always state **conditions** (temperature, time, observer) when quoting any static characteristic.
- ✅ Always express errors as **% of full-scale (FS)** unless the question specifically asks for % of reading.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Errors in Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT611 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Errors in Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Error (in Measurement):** The deviation of a *measured value* from the *true value* of the measurand. Mathematically, $E = M - T$, where $M$ is the measured value and $T$ is the true value. Errors arise because no measurement is absolutely perfect — every instrument, observer, and environment introduces some uncertainty.

**True Value (T):** The ideal value of a quantity that would be obtained by a flawless, perfectly calibrated measurement system. In practice, true value is approached by averaging a large number of measurements or by comparison with primary standards.

**Measured Value (M):** The numerical output obtained when an instrument is used to sense a measurand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Broad Classes of Errors (KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus Mandate):**
> 1. **Gross Errors (Blunders)** — caused entirely by human mistakes.
> 2. **Systematic Errors (Determinate Errors)** — consistent, repeatable, and (in principle) eliminable.
> 3. **Random Errors (Indeterminate / Accidental Errors)** — small, stochastic, irreducible by calibration alone.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are weighing yourself on three different bathroom scales, and your *true* weight (measured at a doctor's clinic using a calibrated beam balance) is **70 kg**.

- **Scale A** always reads **72 kg** → it has a *Systematic Error* (always +2 kg, no matter how many times you stand on it).
- **Scale B** jitters between 69.8, 70.1, 70.3, 69.9… → these tiny fluctuations are *Random Errors*.
- **You** accidentally read 27 kg as 72 kg → that's a *Gross Error* (transcription blip).

Just like the scales, **no transducer or instrument is flawless**. The job of a measurement engineer is to **identify, quantify, and minimize** these errors to a level the application can tolerate (e.g., ±0.1% for a lab balance, ±2% for a domestic scale).

## 1.3 Why Error Analysis Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) emphasizes that an engineer must be able to *quantify* the trustworthiness of a measurement. This topic directly maps to **CO2 (Apply error analysis to measurement data)** and frequently appears as 14-mark analytical problems involving percentage error, limiting error, and combination of errors.

## 1.4 Categories at a Glance

| Class | Nature | Can be eliminated? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Error | Human, single-event | Yes (care, training) | Misreading 53.0 as 35.0 |
| Systematic Error | Deterministic, reproducible | Yes (calibration, correction) | Zero offset in voltmeter |
| Random Error | Stochastic, statistical | No, but reducible by averaging | Thermal noise in sensor |

**Standard metric for error severity:** *Limiting Error* (Guaranteed Accuracy) — the maximum error specified by the manufacturer, expressed as a percentage of full-scale (FS) or of true value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gross Errors (Blunders)

**Operational Concept:** Human-oriented mistakes during data acquisition.

- **Step 1 (Cause):** Misreading of instrument scale, incorrect recording (e.g., 53.0 written as 35.0), incorrect instrument selection.
- **Step 2 (Why):** Fatigue, poor lighting, time pressure, or inexperience of the observer.
- **Step 3 (Mitigation — How):** Repeated readings by multiple observers, careful pre-experiment briefing, and post-experiment data cross-verification. KTU often tags gross errors as "human errors" and expects students to *list at least 3 control measures*.

## 2.2 Systematic Errors (Determinate Errors)

These are *repeatable* and have a *known sign*. Three sub-classes (KTU syllabus mandate):

### (a) Instrumental Errors
- **Zero Error:** Pointer not at zero when measurand is zero. Example: ammeter reads 0.2 A when unconnected.
- **Scale Error:** Scale divisions are not uniform (manufacturing defect).
- **Calibration Error:** Instrument calibrated against a faulty standard.
- **Loading Effect:** Connecting the instrument disturbs the circuit (e.g., voltmeter drawing current).
- **Hysteresis Error:** Output differs for ascending vs. descending input (magnetic hysteresis in iron-core meters).
- **Dead Zone / Backlash:** No output change for small input changes (gears, pointers).
- **Aging Drift:** Gradual change with time.
- **Thermal Drift:** Output shifts due to ambient temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why systematic errors matter most in KTU problems:** They are the *only* class where the **sign of the error can be determined**, allowing **error correction** by calibration curves or zero-adjustment.

### (b) Environmental Errors
- Temperature, humidity, pressure, vibration, and stray magnetic/electrostatic fields alter the measurand or the instrument.
- Example: Strain gauge resistance changes with ambient temperature → requires *temperature compensation*.

### (c) Observational Errors
- Parallax error (reading pointer from an angle), interpolation error between scale marks.
- Eliminated by: mirror-backed scales, digital displays, optical aids.

## 2.3 Random Errors (Indeterminate Errors)

- **Operational Concept:** Small, unpredictable fluctuations arising from many tiny uncontrolled influences (Brownian motion, electronic noise, vibration, micro-variations in contact resistance).
- **Step 1 (Nature):** They follow a *Gaussian (normal) distribution* around the mean.
- **Step 2 (Mathematical Law):** For $n$ independent observations $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$, the most probable value is the arithmetic mean $\bar{x}$.
- **Step 3 (How to handle):** Use **statistical analysis** — mean, standard deviation, probable error — to express uncertainty.

## 2.4 Static vs. Dynamic Errors (KTU Distinction)

- **Static Error:** Error when measurand is steady (time-invariant). Includes calibration, scale, zero, hysteresis, dead-zone, linearity, resolution, and drift.
- **Dynamic Error:** Error when measurand varies with time. It depends on the *dynamic response* of the instrument (rise time, settling time, bandwidth, damping) and is covered under transducer dynamics.

## 2.5 Numerical Quantification of Errors

| Error Type | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Absolute Error | $E_a = M - T$ | Has units; not a fair comparison across ranges |
| Relative Error | $E_r = E_a \div T$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Percentage Error | $E_\% = (E_a \div T) \times 100$ | Most common reporting form |
| Limiting Error (Guaranteed Accuracy) | $E_{lim} = \pm(\%\text{ of FS or True Value})$ | Manufacturer specification |
| Mean Deviation | $\bar{d} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i \vert$ | $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}$ | Most widely used dispersion metric |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ | Square of standard deviation |
| Probable Error (PE) | $PE = 0.6745\,\sigma$ | Error with 50% probability |
| Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_m = \sigma \div \sqrt{n}$ | Reduces as $n$ increases |
| Range | $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Crude dispersion measure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU rule:** Always express $\vert \cdot \vert$ using `\vert` or `\mid` in markdown tables (never the bare pipe `|`) to avoid breaking the table grammar.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

Error analysis is the foundation of **metrology**, **quality control (Six-Sigma)**, **ISO 9001 calibration**, and **uncertainty budgeting** (GUM — Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement). Every certified laboratory, every pressure transmitter in an oil refinery, and every watt-hour meter on your house is rated by these same error metrics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combination of Errors (General Case)

Let a result $R$ be a function of $n$ independent measured quantities $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$:

$$
R = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate (Total Differential).** A small change $dx_i$ in any $x_i$ produces a change $dR$:

$$
dR = \frac{\partial R}{\partial x_1}dx_1 + \frac{\partial R}{\partial x_2}dx_2 + \cdots + \frac{\partial R}{\partial x_n}dx_n
$$

**Step 2 — Replace Infinitesimals with Finite Errors.** In practice, $dx_i \to \pm \Delta x_i$. Since we want the *worst-case (limiting) error*, we take the absolute sum (all signs aligned for maximum magnitude):

$$
\pm \Delta R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{\partial R}{\partial x_i} \right\vert \Delta x_i
$$

**Step 3 — Express as Fractional / Percentage Error.** Divide both sides by $R$:

$$
\pm \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial x_i} \right\vert \Delta x_i
$$

This is the **General Law of Combination of Errors**.

---

## 3.2 Special Cases (KTU High-Yield)

### Case 1 — Sum: $R = A + B$

$$
\Delta R = \Delta A + \Delta B
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta A + \Delta B}{A + B}
$$

### Case 2 — Difference: $R = A - B$

$$
\Delta R = \pm(\Delta A + \Delta B)
$$

**Important:** When $A \approx B$, the *absolute* error may be small while the *relative* error explodes → *loss of significance* (KTU favourite trick).

### Case 3 — Product: $R = A \cdot B$

Take logarithms: $\ln R = \ln A + \ln B$. Differentiate:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dB}{B}
$$

### Case 4 — Quotient: $R = A / B$

$$
\ln R = \ln A - \ln B \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{dR}{R} = \frac{dA}{A} - \frac{dB}{B}
$$

For worst-case magnitude:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm \left( \frac{\Delta A}{A} + \frac{\Delta B}{B} \right)
$$

### Case 5 — Power: $R = A^{n}$

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = n \frac{dA}{A}
$$

### Case 6 — Product-Quotient Chain: $R = A \cdot B / C^{2}$

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm \left( \frac{\Delta A}{A} + \frac{\Delta B}{B} + 2\frac{\Delta C}{C} \right)
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Derivation: Voltmeter Limiting Error

**Problem.** A voltmeter is specified *0–150 V full scale*, guaranteed accuracy $\pm 1\%$ of *full scale*. Find:
(a) the limiting error in volts, (b) the *percentage* limiting error when the meter reads (i) 150 V, (ii) 75 V, (iii) 30 V.

**Solution.**

(a) Limiting error in volts:

$$
E_{lim} = \pm 1\% \times 150 = \pm 1.5 \text{ V}
$$

(b)(i) At 150 V (full-scale reading):

$$
\varepsilon_{\%} = \frac{1.5}{150} \times 100 = 1\%
$$

(b)(ii) At 75 V (half-scale reading):

$$
\varepsilon_{\%} = \frac{1.5}{75} \times 100 = 2\%
$$

(b)(iii) At 30 V (1/5-scale reading):

$$
\varepsilon_{\%} = \frac{1.5}{30} \times 100 = 5\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** *The percentage error rises as the reading falls.* This is why industrial analog meters are operated at **≥ 2/3 of full scale** wherever possible. KTU examiners often ask "why are analog meters preferred at 70–100% of FS?" — the answer is exactly this inverse relationship.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Combination of Resistances

**Problem.** Three resistors are connected in series to form a voltage divider. Their nominal values are $R_1 = 100\,\Omega \pm 5\%$, $R_2 = 200\,\Omega \pm 5\%$, $R_3 = 300\,\Omega \pm 5\%$. Find the limiting error of the total resistance $R = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$.

**Solution.**

Nominal total:

$$
R = 100 + 200 + 300 = 600 \text{ }\Omega
$$

Absolute limiting errors:

$$
\Delta R_1 = 0.05 \times 100 = 5 \text{ }\Omega
$$

$$
\Delta R_2 = 0.05 \times 200 = 10 \text{ }\Omega
$$

$$
\Delta R_3 = 0.05 \times 300 = 15 \text{ }\Omega
$$

Sum rule:

$$
\Delta R = \pm(5 + 10 + 15) = \pm 30 \text{ }\Omega
$$

Percentage error of total:

$$
\varepsilon_{\%}(R) = \frac{30}{600} \times 100 = \pm 5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For *sums of same-percentage* components, the percentage error of the sum equals the common percentage — the low-precision component does *not* degrade the higher-precision one here, but the *absolute* error grows with the magnitude.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Power Dissipation $P = V^{2}/R$

**Problem.** $V = 100\,\text{V} \pm 1\%$, $R = 50\,\Omega \pm 2\%$. Find percentage error in $P$.

**Solution.** Using $P = V^{2}/R$:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{P} = \pm \left( 2\frac{\Delta V}{V} + \frac{\Delta R}{R} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{P} = \pm (2 \times 1\% + 2\%) = \pm 4\%
$$

Nominal $P = 100^2 / 50 = 200 \text{ W}$. Therefore:

$$
\Delta P = \pm 0.04 \times 200 = \pm 8 \text{ W}
$$

So $P = 200 \pm 8$ W.

---

## 3.6 Statistical Analysis of Random Errors (Formula Recap Derivation)

Given $n$ measurements $x_1, \ldots, x_n$:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \quad \text{(Best estimate of true value)}
$$

$$
d_i = x_i - \bar{x} \quad \text{(Deviation of } i^{th} \text{ reading)}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{2}} \quad \text{(Sample standard deviation, Bessel-corrected)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{m} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \quad \text{(Standard error of the mean)}
$$

$$
PE = 0.6745\,\sigma \quad \text{(Probable Error — 50% confidence bound)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU tip:** Use $n-1$ in the denominator for *sample* standard deviation. Use $n$ only when the denominator is *population* standard deviation. Most KTU problems use $n-1$.

---

## 3.7 Calibration — The Cure for Systematic Errors

**Definition:** *Calibration* is the procedure of comparing an instrument's output against a *known standard* (Primary, Secondary, or Working) and deriving a correction factor or curve.

$$
\text{Correction} = \text{True Value} - \text{Indicated Value} = T - M = -E
$$

A **calibration curve** is a plot of *correction vs. indicated value* (or *true vs. indicated*), and the residual scatter of the data points about the best-fit line gives the *random* component.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Measurement Errors

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Measurement Error E equals M minus T] --> nodeB1[Gross Errors Blunders]
    nodeA --> nodeB2[Systematic Errors Determinate]
    nodeA --> nodeB3[Random Errors Indeterminate]

    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[Misreading Scale]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC2[Wrong Recording Transcription]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC3[Wrong Instrument Selection]

    nodeB2 --> nodeD1[Instrumental Errors]
    nodeB2 --> nodeD2[Environmental Errors]
    nodeB2 --> nodeD3[Observational Errors]

    nodeD1 --> nodeE1[Zero Error]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE2[Scale Calibration Error]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE3[Loading Effect]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE4[Hysteresis Dead Zone]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE5[Drift Thermal Aging]

    nodeD2 --> nodeF1[Temperature Humidity]
    nodeD2 --> nodeF2[Stray Fields Vibration]

    nodeD3 --> nodeG1[Parallax]
    nodeD3 --> nodeG2[Interpolation Bias]

    nodeB3 --> nodeH1[Gaussian Distributed]
    nodeB3 --> nodeH2[Handled by Statistics]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH3[Mean Standard Deviation]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH4[Probable Error]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH5[Standard Error of Mean]

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class nodeA root
    class nodeB1,nodeB2,nodeB3,nodeD1,nodeD2,nodeD3,nodeH2 branch
    class nodeC1,nodeC2,nodeC3,nodeE1,nodeE2,nodeE3,nodeE4,nodeE5,nodeF1,nodeF2,nodeG1,nodeG2,nodeH1,nodeH3,nodeH4,nodeH5 leaf
```

## 4.2 Error-Handling Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Measurand Input] --> nodeP2[Transducer and Instrument]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Output Reading M]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4{Error Analysis Stage}
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Identify Systematic Errors]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP6[Quantify Random Errors]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP7[Detect Gross Errors via Re Reading]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP8[Apply Calibration Correction]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP9[Apply Statistical Averaging]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP10[Discard Outliers 3 sigma rule]
    nodeP8 --> nodeP11[Corrected Mean Value]
    nodeP9 --> nodeP11
    nodeP10 --> nodeP11
    nodeP11 --> nodeP12[Report Result with Uncertainty]
```

## 4.3 Combination-of-Errors Logic Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeQ1[Given Function R equals f of A B C] --> nodeQ2{Identify Operation}
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3[Sum or Difference] --> nodeQ3a[Add absolute errors]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ4[Product or Quotient] --> nodeQ4a[Add percentage errors]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ5[Power A to n] --> nodeQ5a[Multiply by n then add]
    nodeQ3a --> nodeQ6[Compute Limiting Error in R]
    nodeQ4a --> nodeQ6
    nodeQ5a --> nodeQ6
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ7[Express as R plus or minus Delta R]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block diagram philosophy:** When physical schematics are too complex to render (as in a real instrument error circuit), the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** above conveys the same pedagogical content for KTU's Module-3 outcomes — error classification, treatment workflow, and algebraic combination logic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define systematic errors. List any four sources of systematic errors in a measurement system.**

**Model Answer:**
Systematic errors are *determinate, reproducible* deviations of the measured value from the true value, having a known and constant sign under repeated measurements of the same quantity. They arise from *defects in instruments*, *environmental influences*, and *observer bias*. Four sources: **(i) Zero error** in analog meters, **(ii) Calibration error** due to faulty standards, **(iii) Loading effect** of voltmeters/ammeters on the circuit, **(iv) Environmental errors** such as ambient temperature drift in strain gauges. *(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 marks for 4 sources.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between Accuracy and Precision in the context of measurement.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Accuracy** is the *closeness of a measured value to the true value* — it is a measure of *systematic* (determinate) error.
- **Precision** is the *closeness of two or more measurements to each other* — it reflects *random* (indeterminate) error and repeatability.
- A measurement can be *precise but inaccurate* (e.g., a miscalibrated digital scale giving 72.1, 72.0, 72.2 kg consistently for a 70 kg person) and *accurate but imprecise* (e.g., readings 69.5, 70.5, 69.8 averaging near 70 kg but with scatter).
- Accuracy is corrected by *calibration*; precision is improved by *averaging many readings*. *(3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-3 Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, every 14-mark question has an *internal choice between two questions from the same module*. Both alternatives below are from **Module 3 — Errors in Measurements**. Each has two 7-mark sub-parts with full model solutions.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a) Classify the different types of errors that occur in a measurement system. Explain the sources of each with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Measurement Errors:**

**1. Gross Errors (Blunders) — Human errors.** These are large deviations caused by human mistakes — misreading a meter, transposing digits while recording (e.g., 53.0 written as 35.0), using the wrong instrument range, or fatigue-induced misjudgement.
*Examples:* Writing 230 V as 23 V; connecting an ammeter in parallel instead of series.
*Elimination:* Multiple readings, cross-checked by another observer, and using digital instruments with auto-ranging.

**2. Systematic Errors (Determinate Errors).** These are *reproducible* and *in principle correctable*. They always push the reading in the *same direction*.
- **(a) Instrumental errors:**
  - *Zero error:* pointer not at zero on no-input.
  - *Scale error:* non-uniform or wrongly printed scale.
  - *Calibration error:* comparison against a faulty standard.
  - *Loading effect:* voltmeter drawing current alters circuit conditions.
  - *Hysteresis error:* different readings for ascending vs. descending input.
  - *Dead zone / backlash:* no output change for small input changes.
  - *Drift:* gradual change with time, temperature, or aging.
- **(b) Environmental errors:** Temperature, humidity, vibration, stray EM fields alter either the measurand or the instrument response. *Example:* Strain gauge zero-shift with temperature.
- **(c) Observational errors:** Parallax when reading pointer at an angle, or personal bias in interpolation between scale marks.
